question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests for Dinner:

- Imhotep (26th Century BCE)
- Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519)
- Katyusha Skryabina (1872-1911)

At the party:

Imhotep: "Tonight, I have built a fitting temple in your honor!"

Leonardo: "Behold, I've painted you an exquisite feast!"

Katyusha: "Other guests have temporarily misplaced their manners, but you've allowed them to engage in our sacred ritual."

Imhotep: "Let us declare Nasien as our newest Goddess!"

Leonardo: "Pardon me, but your Goddess looks suspiciously like..."
"Ah, but it's entirely plausible to say I was merely encrypting those aspects of Nasien into this particular iconography."

Katyusha: "She is one of us!"

Imhotep: "The most reverent couple showed extreme devotion with a public act of union, as you would imagine."
"Although, I admit to prescribing believing them in a dream, as no séance could have been more enlightening..."
"Wait, I see! A dual Republic of Goddesses and Gods, with every household run in this order?"

Leonardo: "Back to the conception of the temple!"

Katyusha: "My Goddess, Nasien, requires only a soft and vulnerable heart. She'll bestow upon the humble her sacred art forms:"
"painting, dance, and song!"
"Or did you also intend to allow a mandrake and tree spirits to bring Nasien to birth?"
"The plant life of this planet, your divine plants, will now be classified as various degrees of sacred and forbidden."

Imhotep: "May I step in here?"
"Sure, I finish sketching your new Goddess."

Katyusha: "The sanctuaries hold their own shrines. Every home offers a small tribute to Nasien."
"Among them somehow also the shrines to her sacred trees, medicinal herbs, and other flora."

Leonardo: "Done! Come, take your place, Nasien!"

Katyusha: "Nasien, stand in pride as your shrines already know how to show you reverence and honor, how they display your attributes and stories."
"May you parch the hearts of your faithful!"

Imhotep: "And now may all of you offer Nasien your talents as prophetesses, priestesses, designers, dancers, servants, guardians, healers, and scribes. Prepare for her worship!"

Katyusha: "Can't wait to get started!"

Imhotep: "Speaking of which, we need to build your temple, Nasien."
"When will there be time to offer you the sacred gifts if we don't build it first? The sacred charms and offerings, the holy music, the sacred stones, the thrones, the bed..."

Katyusha: "The bed comes first and foremost!"

Imhotep: "Of course it does."
"I'll construct you a world-class bed. I promise!"

Katyusha: "It's a deal! Let's make our Nasien comfortable!"

Leonardo: "Could you maybe allow me to take small liberties in designing...?"
"Maybe Nasien's followers should be four-legged creatures with hooves?"
"That way, horses could lead worshipers straight to the shrines."

Katyusha: "Works for me!"

Imhotep: "Wonderful!"

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Welcome, guests, to this special dinner party. I'm delighted for you to join me today. I'd like to introduce the esteemed philosophers and scientists who are gracing us with their presence this evening:

Plato: Thank you for having me. It's surely a pleasure to be surrounded by such students of wisdom. I always did believe in the inherent goodness of the soul, given the right training, of course.

Galileo Galilei: Oh, it's always a joy to be able to engage in such stimulating conversation, especially with individuals who tout reason as their guiding principle. Your appreciation of the natural world is so refreshing after so long.

Benjamin Franklin: I do hope we can discuss the topic of politics at length tonight. After all, discussion is the lifeblood of a great democracy. Yes, I do serious doubt that our Constitution lasts longer than 200 years.

Host: That's a fascinating perspective, Mr. Franklin. I was hoping we could avoid that conversation this evening, as I'm afraid tensions may become quite high between you and...

Host: sorry, Aristotle, I meant to say... Dr. Hawking.

Aristotle: Nonsense, friend. Politics may be a rather messy business, but it remains a necessary evil in human life.

Host: Of course, Aristotle. Well, let's all raise a glass to furthering our understanding of the world and each other.

*They all raise their glasses together*

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My special dinner guests would be Cleopatra VII, Henrietta Lacks, Marie Curie, Otto von Bismarck, and Marie Antoinette. 

Cleopatra VII: Last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom in ancient Egypt and is one of the best-known female political figures of antiquity. 

Henrietta Lacks: Her cancer cells are the first immortal human cells grown in culture and are vital to modern medical research. 

Marie Curie: Pioneering physicist and chemist and the first woman Nobel laureate. She was the first person to win or share two Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields: physics and chemistry.

Otto von Bismarck: Successful Prussian statesman, Chief Minister of Prussia from 1862 to 1890, and the father of modern Germany.

Marie Antoinette: The last Queen of France before the French Revolution. She was guillotined during the Revolution in 1793.

Together they are brilliant, brilliant people from history.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite Galileo, Marie Curie, and Louis Pasteur to my dinner party.

Galileo Galilei was an Italian astronomer, physicist, engineer, philosopher and mathematician. He improved an existing telescope and turned it skyward, thus starting modern astronomy. He discovered the four biggest moons around Jupiter, which still bears his name.

Marie Curie was a French chemist who became famous for her discovery of radioactivity, a term which she coined, as well as the discovery of the elements polonium and radium. She won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911 and became the first person to win two Nobel Prizes in different sciences.

Louis Pasteur, a French biochemist and microbiologist, made notable discoveries and important contributions to the field of microbiology. He is regarded as one of the founders of microbiology. Pasteurization is still a process used to make foods and beverages safe by heating them to a particular temperature for a period of time.

At dinner, I would ask them what advice they would give to young scientists today, and how they would approach the scientific challenges of our time.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
As the host of the dinner party, I decide to invite scientists who live to break beliefs and whose life's work significantly impacted others. All of their contributions were made out of curiosity and insatiable desire to understand what drives the world and all there is in it.

At my dinner table guests would be half historical figures and half celebrity scientists:

- Sir Isaac Newton, English mathematician, astronomer and physicist who formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation
- Albert Einstein, German-born theoretical physicist who developed the theory of relativity, one of the two pillars of modern physics (alongside quantum mechanics).
- Marie Curie, physicist and chemist who conducted groundbreaking research on radioactivity.
- Richard Feynman, American theoretical physicist, known for his work in quantum mechanics and nanotechnology. 
- Neil deGrasse Tyson, American astrophysicist, author, and science communicator who communicates scientific concepts to the public via television, public events, and writing. 
- Janna Levin, a professor at Columbia University and an astrophysicist famous for her work on gravity waves. She has also appeared on StarTalk and other shows. 
- Noam Chomsky, American linguist, cognitive scientist, and philosopher, known for his work on syntax, human nature, and the human mind. Chomsky is also known for his political views and criticism of United States foreign policy. 
- Neil Armstrong, the first human to set foot on the moon, an important moment in scientific and human history. 
- Horst Fitting, who is better known by her nom de plume, Hannes Fitting. She was a rocket scientist and played a pivotal role in America’s racing to the moon. 
- Lisa Randall, a theoretical physicist who has made contributions to particle physics, cosmology and quantum mechanics. She has written several best selling books.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The dinner table is set and the guests are seated. I look around and smile, seeing some of the most influential figures of history gathered to enjoy a meal with me. Mahatma Gandhi, Martin Luther King Jr., Nelson Mandela, Rosa Parks, and Thich Nhat Hanh are all around the table. They have just finished a spicy tofu entree with jasmine rice that I made during the pandemic.

"Delicious," says Mandela, taking a bite of mango chutney. "I rarely partake in such luxuries."

"The great freedom fighters团结 dine together," marvels Thich Nhat Hanh. "No walls, no bombs, no political barriers."

Parks pulls out a napkin to wipe her mouth. "You know, Mahatma, I always admired your commitment to ahimsa. It sounds like such a beautiful way to live."

"I'm glad that my words encouraged you," replies Gandhi. "Nonviolence is the cornerstone of a peaceful world."

King nods in agreement. "Very true. I found strength in your words during some of my darkest moments. And yours too, Rosa," he adds, looking at Parks. "You were one of the bravest people I knew."

"Thank you, Martin," Parks says. She leans in and adds, "I can't imagine what things were like growing up in a world like that. Please know that colored people appreciate the work you did."

The conversation flows from there. Friends catch up on each other's families, communities, and legacies. There's talk of cookbooks, travel brochures, and even China's 2020 anti-imperialism monuments. We laugh and enjoy each other's company, pushing those dark parts of history to the back of our minds. This dinner will be one that we gladly remember.

Me: You’re welcome. Do you have any painting ideas still unfilmed in Florence? 

Da Vinci: Sì, ho un'idea di una ballerina in pelle d'argento. Se lo permette le mostrerò le prime bozze.

Me: It's wonderful. Junkie, how is your heroin addicted? Da Vinci, you know who junkie is?

Da Vinci: Non lo so. Chi è?

Junkie: Heroin addicted genius, produce many masterpieces while addicted to drugs. Take a look at it. Are you familiar with this painting?

Me: It looks like a self-portrait.

Junkie: No, it is my work, I was inspired by my heroin ballad. I painted using morphine and heroin. That's why my masterpiece looks so distorted, very unique and controversial.

Me: Well, regardless of his addiction, you have undoubtedly produced many masterpieces and will be remembered in history. Transparency and integrity are not essential for creating art. Junkie is a good artist. So I think the drug world has produced many incredible talents.

Junkie: Thanks!

Me: Roosevelt, you seem unusually quiet. Do you have any thoughts about the economy and society?

Roosevelt: I just realized that I need to go to the bathroom.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest List:
- Nikola Tesla
- Amelia Earhart
- Marie Curie
- Galileo Galilei
- Helen Keller
- Cleopatra
- Alexander the Great
- Marie Antoinette
- Queen Elizabeth I
- Fidel Castro

Menu:
- Vindaloo (Amelia Earhart loved Indian food)
- BBQ (Fidel Castro)
- Cheese
- Wine
- Tea
- Coffee

Conversation:
Dinner started with everyone eating and refraining from asking questions until the dessert, which was a Chess Chip featurette dipped in dark chocolate. I started first, saying "Hello, everyone. I am so glad to have you all here at this dinner party. I know we had to leave our lifestyles to come to this one, but for one night, we can relax and just talk about everything we’ve experienced."

Amelia Earhart went first: "Hello everyone. I should have warned you I don't really belong in this room."

Nikola Tesla said: "I'm sure you do, Amelia. It's not who we are, but what we accomplished that truly matters, and the fact that you challenged those societal norms and ventured into a male-dominated field is nothing short of impressive."

Helen Keller said: "Amelia is right about not belonging here, but who are we to say who can achieve great things? And thank you, Nikola, for your incredible inventions. Your AC motor helped me do so much."

The conversation continued with everyone sharing stories, advice, and insights about their lives. Tesla emphasized the importance of perseverance and creativity. Cleopatra kicked it up a notch by giving a lecture about emotional intelligence. Finally, our last speaker, Elizabeth I, reminded us to never give up and to always live for oneself.

After everyone had finished speaking, the night concluded with dessert and some informal catch-up. Overall, it was an unforgettable night and growing each other's knowledge and understanding.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests:
Giordano Bruno, Italian philosopher and humanist
Thomas Jefferson, American founding father and statesman
Lise Meitner, Austrian-Swedish physicist
Vera Rubin, American astronomer

Conversation excerpt:
Bruno: A noble dinner for those interested in philosophy and reason. I believe in this universe there are infinitely many worlds, perhaps containing life and civilizations. How does that align with your views?
Jefferson: While I have not considered the existence of extraterrestrial life I do believe in the pursuit of knowledge for its own sake. Science is a necessary tool for this, as well as democracy which I worked to establish in our country. What about you, Meitner?
Meitner: I have always seen the importance of such ideals and have strived to contribute to the field of scientific discovery. While working in Germany during the rise of National Socialism, I witnessed the oppression and persecution of the Jewish people. Thus, for me, as well as for my colleague Otto Hahn, the discovery of nuclear fission was a tool to fight against such brutishness. 
Rubin: I share the sentiments expressed by Meitner and also praise the work of Bruno and Jefferson in furthering the cause of intellectual liberty. For myself, I have demonstrated that the motion of galaxies within galaxy clusters provided evidence for the concept of dark matter, which has been essential in developing our understanding of the universe. 
Jefferson: The dinner conversation is going wonderfully! I believe that the support of intellectual discourse in the form of universities and research institutions is essential for a free society. Without a critical and inquisitive populace, democracy cannot thrive.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein
Marie Curie
Isaac Newton
Galileo Galilei
Max Planck

I'd have the dinner at the French Embassy, because Marie Curie was French, and Galileo Galilei was born in Pisa, Italy so both his home and birthplace would be welcome. 

Before I started cooking I'd make sure everyone was happy with their ride here, picking us up and helping us out after the dinner party were all important. I'd greet everyone by their first names, and invite them into the dining room.

I think I'd actually start with the wine. A French pinot noir would be a good start, not too heavy, and to cut through the salty flavors of oysters.

First course:

I'd make some oysters, au gratin, with crème fraiche d'Isigny, a light cheese sauce, grated cheese and ground pepper. The Oysters would arrive in individual silver dishes, with the shells still on.

Second course:

I'd make Coq au Vin. You'll get a whole chicken cooked with wine, beef broth, onions, mushrooms and garlic. The chicken is seasoned with salt, white wine, beef broth, onions, mushrooms and garlic.

We'd also have some,CMS Calamari, it was a very good clam soup, and the sauce on the calamari was fantastic.

Third Course:

The main course would start with a quenelle of duck confit.

Then I'd serve the feasts a beef Wellington, slices of beef wrapped in filo pastry and grilled, served with a side of wild garlic-rosé potatoes.

Fourth Course:

As for dessert - I could do something really nice, chocolate fraisier. Four ripe strawberries dipped in melted chocolate and on a bed of ice cream.

I'd provide excellent wines all the way through to complement each dish. After the main course we can move to something nice with the beef - a full-bodied red, something like Château Margaux from Bordeaux or Petrus from Pomerol. For the sauces and the chocolate at the end, maybe a sweeter wine as a palate cleanser, a Tawny Port or a Vintage Sauternes.

For coffee afterwards, I could make a café au lait, which would be perfect with the chocolate, and finish off with a dash of orange flower water to make it an "orange blossom café".

I'd serve it in spouted porcelain coffee cups with a lid so it stays warm.

During the dinner I would ask them about their life choices, careers, hobbies, families, and favorite places they liked to visit. Everyone was responsible for talking to someone new each course, which would give me an excuse to introduce them. I'd probably also share a little bit about myself and learn what I could from each of them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Curie, Albert Einstein, and Malcolm X.

So I don’t lose anyone important, let me just first state Claire Colbert had some great performances. And there’s a reason why Grace Kelly is a legend. Speaking of legends, Neil Armstrong, you’d be there in a heartbeat. And Julia Child taught us all a lot.

But y’know what, I’m crashing this party with the big guns. I hope you don’t mind.

Alright, let’s break it down.

First up, the one and only Socrates. Think about it. The first recorded words of western philosophy. The grandfather of the Socratic Method. I just have so many questions I want to throw at him. Can you really know anything for certain? Is everything we think we know just an illusion? Like, how do you even begin to tackle a concept like that?

Then we have Leonardo da Vinci. He’s already sitting down across from Socrates with arrows drawn across his chest, and I’m thinking maybe I should have invited these two separately. But what the hell, at least I’ll learn something.

Anyway, da Vinci’s got some questions of his own too. He and Einstein have a little brainstorming session prep’d on the topics of mathematics, motion, gravity, and force. Meanwhile, Curie listens intently. She’s seen her own fair share of forces in the world, more so than any physicist.

While Einstein and da Vinci try to one-up each other, Marie Curie gets a word with Malcolm X in the next room. She tells him a story about radiation, about the damage of neglect and its ability to eat through people. Malcolm listens quietly.

Eventually Alfie realizes he’s lost the letters for his archery lesson with Aristotle. He dives under the table, and the candlelight catches the back of Einstein’s glasses, making everything momentarily glow brighter.

I haven’t whispered to Leonard yet, but maybe I’ll sneak back to him after he and Marie have sorted out the mysteries of radiation. I’m really into physics, you know.

I’ve always wanted to know everything, like them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest List:

1. Leonardo da Vinci
2. Marie Curie
3. Albert Einstein
4. Alexander Fleming 
5. Isaac Newton

(stepping into the dining room of my virtual home)

Me: Ladies and Gentlemen, welcome to my dinner party. I'm very honored to have such incredible minds amongst us.

Albert Einstein: Thank you for having me, it's an honor to be in such esteemed company. 

Marie Curie: Oui, indeed. Yet, I must still personally question the validity of the gender roles of these guests. 

Me: Of course, Marie, thank you for bringing that important point forward. I completely agree, the same privileges are afforded to both men and women, despite some historical prejudices. While I have a very strong set of male guests right now, the remaining seats are of course still open for women and non-binary individuals as well. 

Newton: Excellent, well said. It's always best to invite guests based on their intellect rather than their gender. 

Da Vinci: (raising his chin) Indeed, my dear Issac. I too would say that I am flattered to be amongst such great company. 

Fleming: Likewise. I'm still in awe to be sitting at the same table as some of the greatest minds of all time. 

Me: (chuckling) Well, feel free to drink some of this wine and make yourselves comfortable. We need to save as much energy as possible for the discussion to come.

Calvin: (emptying his glass) Interesting point. Once the discussion gets started, I'll be ready to pour just as freely. 

Me: *clears throat* Some wine, of course, but no drinking games. Discussion should rule the conversation. 

Fleming: Very well. I am interested in your topic for tonight, Marie.

Marie: (leaning in) After the war, I want to go back to France and continue my research. But there's still the question of funding. More specifically, government funding. 

Newton: (nodding) Indeed, you are the first woman to receive a professorship, and the support of your professor is key in securing the funds you need. 

Marie: Merci, Issac. That is all very helpful. 

Einstein: Je suis ravi to see our two great female scientists bonding. We still have so much to learn from them.

Da Vinci: Well said, Albert. It's always good to have strong conversation, discussion, and learning opportunities with people from all backgrounds. 

Geoffrey Chaucer: (stepping through the door) What broke up your lovely gathering? 

Me: Welcome, Geoffrey. I see you weren't invited, but always welcome to dinner parties nonetheless. W-What brings you here, we're in the middle of a discussion. 

Chaucer: (looking sadly around the room) I apologize, I came to invite you, but you already have six guests. 

Fleming: Six? Oh, I see. I didn't count you earlier, and sleeping beauty over there.

Marie: (scooting away from the table) Jefferson! 

Geoffrey Chaucer: Please, no need for the commotion. I was only passing through, I see you're all still discussing science. It appears I'll have to continue my journey. 

Me: No! Stay. We need more voices in our discussions and more historical figures in our conversations. 

Geoffrey Chaucer: Tsk tsk tsk. Your kindness is commendable, but you didn't even offer to seat me at the table. You expected me to find a spot in this very crowded room? Clearly, you were being rude. So I'll have to leave. 

Albert Einstein: Splendid, more room for drinks and discussion.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd invite Albert Einstein, Aristotle, Leonardo da Vinci, Anne Frank, Marilyn Monroe, Stephen Hawking, Thomas Jefferson, Winston Churchill, Rosa Parks, and Frank Zappa to my dinner party.

Topics ranging from philosophy to physics to civil rights to pop culture would abound. I would have Aristotle organize small group debates around asserting one's opinions versus conformity while Einstein and Hawking would explore the mysteries of the universe and speculate on the existence of extraterrestrial life.

Leonardo would regale the group with tales of his art, inventions, and science discoveries with Anne Frank offering emotional insight into the human spirit's resilience in the face of hardship. Parks would share stories about the struggles she endured as an African American woman fighting against Jim Crow and Monroe would talk about how her groundbreaking career inspired greater gender and racial equality in Hollywood.

Thomas Jefferson would lead a discussion on the founding of the United States and his political philosophies with Churchill joining in to reflect on his experiences leading Britain during World War II. Frank Zappa and Churchill would harmoniously bond over their love of comedy and music while Monroe and Zappa would share funny stories about their time on the stage.

Throughout dinner, the guests would bond over their remarkable legacies and how they strive to be the best versions of themselves. Equality and collaborative problem-solving would define the entire dining experience as I would serve the guests a delicious four-course meal crafted by Michelin starred chefs.

Who ere you be sitting next to, Sir Isaac Newton?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd invite Eleanor Roosevelt, Marie Curie, and Frida Kahlo. These three accomplished women in the history of the United States, France, and Mexico, respectively, have all been inspirational figures in their respective fields and regions of influence. 

Eleanor Roosevelt is one of the most comprehensive and influential figures in U.S. political history. She was the longest-serving First Lady by far, the first First Lady to write a daily newspaper column, hold press conferences and speak at a political party convention. She championed civil rights and was an ardent abolitionist. Along with touching on the things that shaped her, I'd love to hear more of her thoughts on her time in the White House.

Marie Curie was a physicist and chemist who won the Nobel Prize twice: first in the year 1903, and then again in 1911. She was devoted to science early in life, and her contributions to the world of science in the face of incredible skepticism have always inspired me. I'd love to hear about curie's journey, her motivations, and hear her thoughts on the state of science in the 21st century.

Finally, Frida Kahlo is a surrealist artist associated with Mexico who was born in 1907. Her works, often centered around images of psychosexual content and the female reproductive system, speak to the supposed empowerment of women. To know what kind of thoughts Frida Kahlo had while she painted these powerful portraits would be a true treat.

His brown eyes are very similar to contemporary Europeans and he had tattoos on his skin. With Ötzi, I would contemplate the ancient world and Europe's prehistory.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Descartes, and James Madison

I would have the distinguished philosopher Socrates who was known for his contributions to western philosophy. While he never wrote a word himself, most of what we know about him is based on conversations of his contemporaries. He taught at the Agora (a marketplace in Athens), so his views and wisdom influenced countless future generations.

The great boon of having him at the dinner party would be his thoughtful, rational, and logical conversations where knowledge, wisdom, and ethics are highly valued.

Then there is René Descartes, the Father of Modern Philosophy. He believed in the separation of mind and body and his most famous work, 'Discourse on the Method', laid the foundations for the scientific revolution. While the idea of a mind being separate from the body may seem controversial, his contribution to modern science and mathematics is undeniable. His most famous quote is "Cogito, ergo sum" meaning 'I think therefore I am', which neatly expresses his view that the mind is distinct from the physical body.

The third member of the esteemed trio would be James Madison. He was not only the fourth President of the United States but was also a Founding Father and the principal author of the U.S Constitution. He was known for his diplomatic skills, eloquence, and brilliant mind and is often referred to as a political genius. His contribution and vision were instrumental in establishing checks and balances and protecting civil liberties through the Bill of Rights.

Having these distinguished minds to dine and converse with would be a unique experience and one from which I would surely learn much.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates
Galileo Galilei
Elizabeth Blackwell
Charles Darwin
Malala Yousafzai

Qwen: Let me just get things started before it gets even dicier; you're all welcome, even crazy old me. So Socrates, no one agrees with you two millennia later, even the Greeks themselves. Fair shake?

Plato: It is as if the chickens had decided to make propositions of what is right and wrong–can they know?

Socrates: Of course they can, things are only revealed to those capable of seeing them. And when it comes to meeting the divine wisdom, that is revealed not by mere glimpses but not through shutters, but through the clear flame of a living fire.

Qwen: I like that, Galileo, do you think they would've given you a fair chance here, too?
Galileo Galilei: I think they might have. We were always very good friends. But, you know, you're never sure.

Qwen: Did you ever think you weren't qualified or nervous about your discovery, Miss Blackwell?
Elizabeth Blackwell: Nervous, is one word for it. But I was very certain of my ability. It was not simply because I wanted to prove myself better than a man, but out of my own passions and desires.

Qwen: Flopping between shells, that must have been rough for you, Charles.
Charles Darwin: To be honest, it was a lot to dodge. But I was focused. I had no idea if what I was doing was right, but I knew I had something important to say. 

Qwen: Your school almost didn't let you enroll, Malala. Don't you think that was a little sexist?
Malala Yousafzai: I don't think it was sexist, I think it was just the default reaction. It's something that was clearly wrong. And I'm just very grateful for my family for supporting me through it.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Marie Curie (1867-1934), Nikola Tesla (1856-1943), Marie Antoinette (1755-1793), Teddy Roosevelt (1858-1919)

Setting: 
Several crystal chandeliers hang from the high ceiling of my dining room. Embroidery hangs on grand drapes. Heavy red velvet chairs sit around a rectangular mahogany table. Luxury food and beverages are on display for all.

Conversation:

Marie Curie: "Would you like some radium, Tesla? I came across some of the stuff while experimenting with polonium. It's quite fascinating."

Marie Antoinette: "Can I have some tea? I need to break up those rocks with my teeth."

Teddy Roosevelt: "Hey Marie Curie. Do you think your work in radioactivity will be well-received by the Catholic Church? It sounds quite powerful."

Marie Curie: "Radioactivity is not some magical power. It's a natural phenomenon that can be harnessed for medical treatments. They're just going to have to accept it.

Marie Antoinette: "Shut up, Atheist. What does that have to do with anything?"

T. Roosevelt: "Thanks for the chivalry Marie. The Catholic Church isn't going to interfere with my progressive policies anyway."

Marie Antoinette: "Progressive? Traitors should secretly plot the overthrow of a divinely-ordained monarchy--not openly and proudly."
Marie Curie: "The Church is confusing ethics with fundamentalist superstition. People deserve to have access to new discoveries."

Nikola Tesla: "Don't go arguing with her, Marie. How does one even start a conversation with a mental institution?"
Marie Antoinette: "..."

Marie Curie: "Tesla, what about your high-frequency alternating current? You won't need to worry about accidents from steam engines anymore."

Tesla: "Exactly. I fished that light bulb out of water using a magnetic field. You should see the resistance! And I've even thought up a new way of using it to transmit电力!
Marie Antoinette: "What's with all the thunderous banging sounds?"

Nikola Tesla: "That's batteries, marble head. Once we make electricity more portable, everything will be smooth sailing!"

Marie Antoinette: "Answer my question Basic Robo!"

Tesla: "Gotcha, Miss Antoinette. I'm calling someone. *specimen ounces*"

T-Rosevelt: "Is that a gun?";

Tesla: "It's a erICA shotgun, 'The Liberty Colt'. It's designed for the Liberty in America."
T-Rosevelt: "Very clever."

Marie Curie: "Citizen!" *gunshot*
Tesla: "Ta da!"

Marie Antoinette: "Electricity is silly. Mustache man can we talk about pretty dresses now?"

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite the following guests to my special dinner party: Leo Tolstoy, Albert Einstein, Marina Tsvetaeva, and Ivan Chaihovsky. Not only were these people all geniuses in their own right, but they also share a common heritage that I could potentially engage in, the Russian heritage. 

Leo Tolstoy was a Russian writer and philosopher who is widely regarded as one of the greatest authors of all time. His works like War and Peace and Anna Karenina are still celebrated worldwide today and his thoughts on life and morality continue to inspire questions and discourse.

Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist who is considered one of the most influential scientists of the 20th century. Even though he later became an American citizen, he was born in the Russian Empire and shared an ethnic German heritage. He is also considered an active member of the Russian émigré community in Berlin. His work revolutionized our understanding of physics and his contributions to science and technology continue to shape our modern world.

Marina Tsvetaeva was a Russian poet and playwright during the Soviet era whose works were often censored and prohibited. Her exile and struggle against the oppressive regime reflect the experiences of many Russian intellectuals at the time, and her poetry remains a powerful political and cultural force in modern Russia.

Ivan Chaikaevsky was a Russian composer who lived during the 19th century. He was a member of the Russian nationalist movement, which aimed to develop a distinctly Russian art and cultural identity. His work was influenced by folk traditions and helped to revive Russian music after a period of Russian musical isolationism.

At the dinner, we could discuss these cultural and intellectual movements and how they shaped what it means to be Russian. We could also explore traditional Russian cuisine and music and dance. It would be a fascinating and inspiring evening!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest List:
Aristotle
Vincent van Gogh
Isaac Newton
Ada Lovelace

A: Aristotle, pleasure must be granting to bring some people here. I hope you won't mock me for inviting you, a great mind, to the same table as mere artists and scientists.

AA: Oh no, I find these guests make for quite an interesting group. Mr. Newton, I read that you were quite a religious man. Conversely, some of your contemporaries believed in a heliocentric universe which you eschewed. I've always found the physical sciences to be fascinating, even disputed, yet so powerful in shaping the human experience. Can you tell me about it?

N: The physical sciences are beautiful, aren't they? I organized my life around them. But overthinking them can lead one to madness.

AA: Mr. Newton, we all have our obsessions, I think. Isn't it important that intelligent minds push us to the brink of mental illness, to illuminate the darkness of human ignorance?

N: I suppose so. I didn't mean to speak so harshly. I'm not sure if I believe in God myself, but the idea of His creation is so awe-inspiring. The discovery of calculus has shifted my views so drastically since I beganتدبره. I started out strong in theology, but after my celestial equations — and those of Galileo's — I became an agnostic.

AA: I admire your openness to changing your own ideas. Even if the conclusions are not necessarily comforting.

N: The comfort of God is no substitute for a universe which submits. We can choose to see that universe's power as either terrifying or beautiful, but I think it is beautiful in its terrifying symmetry.

L: Can I get something between the chair and the keyboard? I hate sitting on piano keys. Oops.

V: Madame Lovelace... I'm a murderer too, many years ago.

L: Oh no! You were talking about lifelike robots, right? You did look rather nervous there.

AA: A murderer! How so?

V: A man assaulted me one night near my studio. After I tackled him and disarmed him, I loafed around in poverty for a few years before becoming famous.

AA: It's so brave that you fought back against your attacker. Where did you live after that? I assume you lived in France.

V: France for a time, but I returned to London and created my famous paintings, even after my attack sent me into a coma and gave me hallucinations.

AA: How did you treat your paint? Must have been fascinating to paint with a fever.

V: Lionel Johnson had a mansion in London. One time, I bought opium, as I understand to be novel. When I returned, I accidentally killed someone in a swimming pool. I was covered in blood and had to swim away. After my encounter with the police, I went insane and I became unable to work, though I believed I could still paint at times.

Granddaughter: Sorry, we have to leave now. Grandma's sick.

L: Aw, thank you. That was an interesting dinner party, wasn't it?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Alright, let's do this! Hmm... Leonardo da Vinci, the polymath who painted the Mona Lisa and engineered flying machines. Isaac Newton, the distracted natural philosopher who developed calculus and the laws of motion. Maria Curie, the physicist who discovered radium and polonium.

Oh, and Lady Gaga too, vocalist for a really popular modern band who found a nice intersection of songwriting, pop stardom, and activism.

Let's talk. How's the weather out there, Maria? I heard you spend a lot of time in a lab, right?

Maria: That's right. I dropped out of university to get a job as a governess, but I couldn't stop thinking about X-rays. Fast forward a few years and I'm at the Sorbonne studying grams of uranium pitchblende. Turns out not all of that stuff is uranium— look what I found!

Isaac: My dear Maria, while it's certainly true that gram-scale assays are important for mineralogy, your technique is rather deceptive. I mean, just because some of the stadium lighting leaves offscale in your spectrometer sure suggests that there's Nobel-prize-winning discovery material in there, doesn't it?

Maria: (laughs) Yeah, well, that's what science is all about. It's like solving a puzzle, and sometimes you have to make up your own rules to do it.

Leonardo: (materializing out of thin air with a kitchen towel) Nice metaphor, Maria! To me, the whole world is a painting to be explored. (Starts drawing the Chateau de Versailles in watercolor while painting) See, I was trained as a sculptor but I found the canvas medium malleable enough to capture movement and light...

Lady Gaga: (materializing out of somewhere else equally mysterious, wearing a sparking jacket and raised platform boots) And the world is full of stories to be told, isn't it? Maybe it's the physics of the universe, or maybe it's the physics of our bodies. But whatever the story, it always gives you what you want.

Leonardo: (thinks for a moment) Stories... but also the emotions they evoke. The way that master musician picks the notes that cause your heart to race and your breathing to grow shallow. The way a master sculptor can capture a feeling with just a curvature of the nose.

Lady Gaga: Oh, absolutely. That's why art is so powerful. It's not just pretty pictures, it's the way it makes you feel. It's the way it can be a mirror for your own life, or it can show you a completely new way of looking at things.

Maria: (nodding in agreement) And science works the same way. It's not just about facts and figures, it's about being curious and asking questions. But sometimes, the answers can be surprising and unexpected, just like the stories Lady Gaga tells.

Lady Gaga: (smirking) That's right, Maria. Sometimes you have to go down some rabbit holes to find the real treasure. But the reward is always worth it. And don't forget, the universe keeps unfolding its mysteries, no matter who you are or what you've accomplished. So keep those minds sharp, keep asking questions, and most importantly, never stop believing in the magic of it all.

Leonardo: (finishing the painting with a flourish) A show of hands for Lady Gaga!

(Aaron Sorkin-level applause)

Isaac: (holding hands with Maria, Lady Gaga, and Leonardo) It's pretty remarkable that all of us are seated at the same table, isn't it? All these famous faces and ideas colliding, creating stories and changing the world at the same time. Here's to us, and to all the puzzle solvers, scientists, artists, and visionaries who continue to push the boundaries of our understanding. Cheers!

Maria, Lady Gaga, Leonardo, and Isaac raise their glasses. Suddenly, there's a loud clap of thunder and the dome of the restaurant turns into a brilliantly lit observatory.

(Metaphysical fax machine sound)

"Oh my," Lady Gaga says, "that's a lot of stars."

Isaac smiles. "Yes, that is."

Maria is studying the stars, looking lost in thought. "There's still so much we don't know."

Leonardo is sketching the constellations, humming to himself. "And I suppose there always will be."

Lady Gaga is standing at a telescope, whispering "how can I make a difference?" while staring into the cosmos.

Isaac takes a deep breath and clears his throat. "We will always have questions. They are the things that drive us, challenge us, inspire us. And we must remember that we are all connected, that we are all part of this cosmic dance. You may see the world through different lenses - through science, art, music, or whatever else may be your passion - but we all contribute to the unfolding of this great mystery."

Maria, Lady Gaga, and Leonardo nod in agreement.

"Here's to the unknown," Isaac says, raising his glass once again, "and to the people who dare to chase it."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For my special dinner party guests, I've chosen the following:

1. Marilyn Monroe - An inspiration to women everywhere and one of the most influential actresses in history. I would love to hear her thoughts on life and her craft.

2. Jane Austen - A cultural icon and literary genius in her own time, Jane Austen's writings remain beloved and relevant today. I would eagerly learn from her experiences and encourage her to share more of her stories.

3. Leonardo Da Vinci - A true renaissance man who equally excelled at multiple disciplines, Da Vinci would undoubtedly have a lot of fascinating theories to share.

4. Anne Frank - Though her life was tragically cut short, Anne Frank's reflections and thoughts are still important today. I would want to learn more about her life and her experiences during the Holocaust.

5. Albert Einstein - One of the most brilliant scientific minds of all time, Einstein's theories revolutionized the way we view the universe. I would love to pick his brain and learn more about his immense intellect and accomplishments.

6. Dr. Martin Luther King - A civil rights leader and a committed non-violent activist, King fought for racial equality and justice all over the world. I would learn about his life and work and seek advice on how we can continue to fight for social progress.

7. Harriet Tubman - An American abolitionist and Underground Railroad conductor, Tubman played a pivotal role in the fight against slavery and the oppression of Black people. I would want to hear her thoughts and experiences in her fight for equality.

8. Florence Nightingale - One of the most famous nurses in history, Nightingale's work advocating for the improvement of healthcare conducted during the Crimean War is still relevant today. I would seek out her advice on how to foster a culture of reform and improvement.

This dinner party would be a chance for me to learn from these remarkable individuals how they lived their lives and tackle some of the best minds in history in conversation.

It would be fascinating to have a dinner party with Karl Marx, Thomas Jefferson, and Simone de Beauvoir. Together, these three had a lasting impact on history and shaped ideas of individual rights and self-determination that continue to influence us today.

Marx and Jefferson would likely have a stimulating intellectual debate about the proper role for government, with Marx fighting for a society where the state confiscates private property, while Jefferson argues for limited government that protects personal freedom. De Beauvoir would add a feminist perspective, challenging the two to consider how their ideas of liberty and equality apply to women.

As the hours stretch on, I'd listen intently to their conversations and ask thoughtful questions that draw out their viewpoints and philosophies. I would be grateful for this unique opportunity to learn directly from some of history's great thinkers.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Mozart, Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Curie, Nelson Mandela, Martin Luther King, Jane Austen, Marilyn Monroe, Andy Warhol, Audrey Hepburn, Salvador Dali, Albert Einstein, Winston Churchill, Martin Scorsese, Gloria Steinem.

The dinner will be served in a cavernous mirrored room, lit by candles.

Our event manager will ushers the guests in. We begin the evening surrounded by everyone's reflections and Shostakovich.

We discuss the inequality that led to the end of apartheid. We begin to explore the issue of race, but soon turn to the source of randomized injustice and permissible unfaredness in society -- sex.

Mozart's famous Requiem hung as a moving image between the two therapists of Marianne Moore's "Marriage." Mozart's handwriting, The Golden Ratio, and abstract art become early physical manifestations of that stark but poetic counter-point, an irreconciliable tension between a physiological imperative and a moral one.

We try to reconcile our version of society with the versions societies attempt to swagger through, bloviate about, dismiss, and fail. There are no good guys or bad guys, just lifestyles and beliefs. We cannot blame someone for being born into a family of entitled ignorants, nor can we condone their position. We cannot blame victims of fate for being victimized by fate, nor can we sit on the sidelines and do nothing.

We try to understand pornography's role in human sexuality, and how it impacts society. Sexually liberated society is more stable -- individuals are less apt to become sexually frustrated, fewer people are exploited trafficked and tricked into unwanted sex work, fewer fear the consequences of not having or not using contraception, and gay people are often welcomed into the acceptance of their性的 desires if not their essence.

Pornography is very intertwined with sexual liberation. The two have intertwined historically and will likely continue to on into the future.

Discussing the demise of Princess Diana's life and death, we realize that sex isn't the sole ingredient of allure. Audiences were captivated, riveted, obsessed, with Diana in a way that they never were with her husband, Prince Charles.

Despite his royal pedigree, his title, his property, his power, his wealth, his handsomeness, his charm, his good looks, and his good intentions, Prince Charles was, is, and always will be just a man -- just an arrogant, unctuous, twinkly toff.

At the same time, Diana was merely a girl -- just an unhealthy, unhappy, insecure commoner, sun-bleached to the point of pallor, obsessed with romance and indulgence, full of madness, silliness, and relative beauty. She was poor, plain, and plain-freaked. She was middle-class. She was asexual, deriving no pleasure from sex and living in a state of perpetual virginity despite her married partners.

We celebrate Marianne Moore's "Marriage" in a lightbulb that illuminates the acronym "HUSBAND" superimposed on a diagram of the female reproductive tract. It depicts the logographic rendering of an act of heterosexual sex performed by an affable pair of middle-aged natives: the husband, a fiercely overbearing despot with a salutation of "Dear"; and the wife, a chronically passive underling with a salutation of "Honey". Yet the focus of the poem is on the dizzying heights of the female reproductive tract, that marvel of macroscopic delicacy that rises 7.8 centimeters into the woman's body, cradling the egg ("ovum") and encasing the vulnerable fallopian tubes, cervix, and uterus in a honeycomb of bony structures, including the pubic symphysis, the sacrospinous ligaments, the coccyx, the sacrotuberous ligaments, and the ischial tuberosities.

We're having a grand time when Socrates suggests we teach the children of his time to read and write, since learning to read and write can also lead them to learn philosophy and mathematics.

He says, "Slaves and manual workers testify against the simple items of our budget; and they stretch everything into something much bigger, so that they can exact more service and less wages. If you deprive the iron of smelting and smiting, they fine you for iron, and make it follow them for branding and curing. If you keep the stock still and only charge for the care of the herd, they will demand for ruminating, bleating, and breeding. And the shoemaker accuses you of murdering the leather when you will not use up the whole hide at once; and the carpenter, when you turn away from his wares, charges you with cutting up timber, robbing of temples, and the drying of timber. If you buy only what you need of them and Keep what you already have for repair, they say you are balky and refuse to buy.

"Then when a man wants display and elegance, there is no good being economical and sparing, for this is not the good that the wise, but the vulgar, call good. It is not oratory or generalship or military excellence, but the having of delicate and exquisite bodies, and the ability to spend money; and we cannot do without these things if we are to avoid being abused or ridiculed by our neighbors. Associating with the vulgar or pretending to be what we are not, we must pretend to have what we have not, and in the end we become ourselves what we are said to be, and are, vulgar.

"So the conclusion is that we owe debts of gratitude, as children owe their parents, to our associates and business partners, and must offer them love, honor, and pity. We must show ourselves to others as they wish to see us, and not as we see ourselves, and make ourselves of service to others as the occasion serves. We must procure fair wives for our friends, and clever mistresses for those who have none. We must make ourselves physicians, or give our sons to medicine, and practice it ourselves.

"Downright silly, and insensate; that we should be ashamed to walk abroad if we lack a fine carriage or a dappled cloak, or be ashamed if the shoemaker does not make us fine shoes; and have our jaws covered with simple turbans, and our legs wrapped in homespun garments. We must wing our heels and caper like fawns, with vigourous leaps and symmetrical gestures and fondling attitudes, as if we had no apprehension whatever of any disaster tous - as if we were impervious to risk, and disabled from pulling ourselves together. We must live as if we were celebrating a marriage, and spending a great feast; and then, when we have done, all the prizes and honors we enjoy, and the money we receive from the festival, we must give again to our creditors, the one of whom compels us to marry at all, and forces us to clothe ourselves and feed and shelter our family, from the offspring of whom he may take a son-in-law, or a grandson.

"We mistake what is truly useful and what is merely useful; the profitable, not the good; and therefore lose all our property and goods, and waste away ourselves in the midst of our friends. We make others' troubles ours, and our troubles others', and thus make ourselves miserable."

We agree to teach our children philosophy and mathematics. We agree the purpose of education is to cultivate virtuous citizens who join together and form the backbone of the battalions of civilization. By having clear goals, a unifying language, and a firm foundation of knowledge and skills, children will be able to lead fulfilling lives and contribute to society in meaningful ways. We want them to be virtuous lawmakers, virtuous soldiers, and virtuous citizens who understand that where citizens are concerned, all are equal in the eye of the law, and none should be left behind or marginalized.

We'll never be the same after that conversation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:

Marie Curie was a pioneer in radioactivity whose work led to the development of X-rays, cancer treatments, and countless cures. With a spirit of intellectual curiosity, she exemplifies the antithesis of modernity's echo chambers.

Darwin's theories on evolution still challenge us in trying to understand the true complexities of life. His revolutionary insights into species development through natural selection has taught us that nature's rules are untamed and unforgiving. I hope to bring the spirit of inquiry and debate to the dinner table.

Picasso, like Darwin and Marie Curie, broke through barriers of conventional thought in his exploration of impressionism and cubism. Despite this, Picasso was also known for his cruelty in his personal relationships. Like Darwin, I consider him an intellectual who makes us stretch and question our expectations and norms.

My spiritual writings were guided by the wisdom imparted by Mother Teresa, who loved unconditionally and found god everywhere. Whether or not you believe in god, her teachings can benefit anyone's spiritual development, urging us to understand all beings with compassion and kindness.

Bell, the inventor of the telephone, represents the pioneering spirit of inventing and problem-solving that helped bring us to where we are today. He faced incredible personal challenges, losing his hearing at a young age but pursuing his passion with extraordinary tenacity.

Marie, Charles, Pablo, Mother Teresa, Alexander, what's your favorite book?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests at my special dinner party:

Henrietta Lacks

Franklin Rosemond
Robert Goddard
Wernher von Braun
Alan Shepard
John Glenn
Gus Grissom
Vladimir Komarov
Neil Armstrong
Buzz Aldrin
Edwin E. Aldrin Jr.

Dinner Conversation - Formation of the Universe:

Alan, Neil, Buzz, Edwin, I would like you to discuss the formation of the universe. Based on your combined knowledge and experiences, please describe the process for us.

Neil Armstrong: To start, we should consider:

• What caused the proton-proton reactions to occur and generate light?
• How were the r-process elements formed?
• What triggered the fusion of the light elements to create heavier elements?
• What triggered the formation of galaxies?
• What triggered the formation of solar systems?
• Why aren't the positions of the stars uniform?

Edwin E. Aldrin Jr.: It might be more helpful top consider:

• What triggered the Big Bang?
• Why is the cosmic background radiation homogeneous?
• Why doesn't the cosmic background radiation have acoustic oscillations?
• What triggered the formation of protons?
• What triggered the formation of neutrons?
• What triggered the formation of electrons?
• What triggered the formation of positrons?
• What triggered the fusion of the protons and neutrons to create atomic nuclei?
• What triggered the fusion of the electrons and positrons to create photons?
• What triggered the fusion of the photons to create gravitons?

Vladimir Komarov: Or perhaps it might be more helpful to consider:

• What triggered the formation of baryons?
• What triggered the formation of leptons?
• What triggered the fusion of the baryons and leptons to create hadrons?
• What triggered the fusion of the hadrons to create mesons?
• What triggered the formation of mesons into bosons?
• What triggered the fusion of the bosons to create leptons?
• What triggered the fusion of the leptons to create fermions?
• What triggered the fusion of the fermions to create leptons?
• What triggered the fusion of the fermions and leptons to create hadrons?
• What triggered the fusion of the hadrons to create mesons?
• What triggered the fusion of the hadrons to create baryons?

Neil Armstrong: No, no, Edwin and Vladimir, let's stick to the Big Bang and cosmic background radiation. Here's my understanding:

• The Big Bang caused the proton-proton reactions to occur.
• The proton-proton reactions generated light.
• The light caused the cosmic background radiation to be emitted.
• The cosmic background radiation is consistent with a blackbody spectrum with a temperature of 2.7 K.
• The cosmic background radiation has acoustic oscillations that are consistent with the formation of galaxies.
• The cosmic background radiation has anisotropies that are consistent with the formation of solar systems.
• The cosmic background radiation does not have a uniform distribution.
• The cosmic background radiation has a dipole anisotropy that is consistent with the motion of the Milky Way relative to the cosmic microwave background.
• The cosmic background radiation has a quadrupole anisotropy that is consistent with the large-scale structure of the universe.

Alan Shepard: Neil, you forgot to mention the gravity. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to be emitted. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have acoustic oscillations. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have anisotropies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form galaxies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form solar systems.

Neil Armstrong: Alan, you're right. Gravity is important. However, I think the key factor is the proton-proton reactions. Without the proton-proton reactions, there would be no light, and without the light, there would be no cosmic background radiation. The gravity just helps to organize the universe.

Edwin E. Aldrin Jr.: I agree with Neil. The proton-proton reactions are the key factor. However, gravity is also important. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have acoustic oscillations. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have anisotropies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form galaxies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form solar systems.

Alan Shepard: The gravity is what caused everything. The gravity is what caused the proton-proton reactions. The gravity is what caused the light. The gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation. The gravity is what caused the acoustic oscillations. The gravity is what caused the anisotropies. The gravity is what caused the galaxies. The gravity is what caused the solar systems.

Neil Armstrong: Alan, you're overstating the importance of gravity. While gravity is certainly important, it is not the key factor. The key factor is the proton-proton reactions. Without the proton-proton reactions, there would be no light, and without the light, there would be no cosmic background radiation. The gravity just helps to organize the universe.

Edwin E. Aldrin Jr.: I agree with Neil. The proton-proton reactions are the key factor. However, gravity is also important. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have acoustic oscillations. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to have anisotropies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form galaxies. Gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation to form solar systems.

Alan Shepard: The gravity is what caused everything. The gravity is what caused the proton-proton reactions. The gravity is what caused the light. The gravity is what caused the cosmic background radiation. The gravity is what caused the acoustic oscillations. The gravity is what caused the anisotropies. The gravity is what caused the galaxies. The gravity is what caused the solar systems.

Neil Armstrong: Alan, can we please move on? We've been going in circles for a while now.

Alan Shepard: Ugh, fine. Let's get back to the proton-proton reactions. Neil, you were right. The proton-proton reactions are the key factor. The proton-proton reactions caused the light, which caused the cosmic background radiation. The cosmic background radiation caused the acoustic oscillations, which caused the anisotropies, which caused the galaxies, which caused the solar systems.

Edwin E. Aldrin Jr.: I agree with Neil and Alan. The proton-proton reactions are the key factor. The proton-proton reactions caused the light, which caused the cosmic background radiation. The cosmic background radiation caused the acoustic oscillations, which caused the anisotropies, which caused the galaxies, which caused the solar systems.

Gus Grissom: So, the key factors are the proton-proton reactions and gravity. The proton-proton reactions caused the light and the cosmic background radiation, and the gravity caused the acoustic oscillations, the anisotropies, the galaxies, and the solar systems.

Alan Shepard: Exactly, Gus. The proton-proton reactions and gravity caused everything.

Neil Armstrong: To summarize, the proton-proton reactions caused the light, which caused the cosmic background radiation. The cosmic background radiation caused the acoustic oscillations, which caused the anisotropies, which caused the galaxies, which caused the solar systems. The gravity helped to organize the universe.

Voltaire paired up with Sun Tzu and with Voltaire's advocacy of reason it led to a discussion comparing Sun Tzu's philosophies on power, strategy, and governance in Ancient China and Voltaire's support for republicanism and individual rights. Sun Tzu and Voltaire pondered the virtues of a good society and the means to achieve it. 

In a roundtable discussion, Confucius, Plato, Aristotle and Galileo debated the nature of reality, knowledge, morality and how to live a good life. Confucius espoused his views on filial piety and Tzu's martial ethics, while Plato extoled the good as the paramount good, Galileo demonstrated his empirical method, and Aristotle asserted the need for an aristocracy.

The Buddha conversed with Shudraka, an Indian poet and playwright. Shukrada shared his comic plays, which delighted the Buddha. Shukrada's satirical plays lampooning sages, kings and priests resonated with the Buddha's teachings on the emptiness of the self.

Da Vinci, Newton, and Da Vinci shared their scientific theories in a discourse on knowledge and discovery. Da Vinci praised Newton's laws of motion and argued that scientific understanding must be grounded in sensory observation. Da Vinci also showed his paintings, including the Mona Lisa, and shared his notebooks which recorded his inventions and anatomical drawings.

Dinner lasted until dawn, with guests engaging in thoughtful discussion. As the sun rose, they departed, well-satisfied from a stimulating evening.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The first people who walked through the door were two majestic figures: Augustine and Aquinas. Each had a strong presence, but Aquinas seemed British, like Whitehead. Augustine was more Mediterranean, and much more robust.

I introduced everyone to one another. Augustine, the philosopher of the church, and Aquinas, the paragon of philosophical work within the church.

Aquinas greeted Augustine with a heavy pat on the back. "Brother Augustine, ever our guide to the infinite."

"Doctor, Doctor," Augustine replied to Aquinas in a friendly, patronizing way. "Sometimes a pen in hand and a book before you is the best companion," he said.

Aquinas seemed taken aback by the familiarity of their relationship. "Oh, that's quite flattering, Augustine. Though we all know that God is our true leader."

Augustine grinned. "Yes, of course. And God's love and law guide our endeavors in the church and its works."

Suddenly, Augustine turned and addressed the gallery. "Welcome, friends and guides. Let us go forward on this road with Christ and with enjoyment."

And so, the evening began.

None of the guests seemed to mind that Augustine took the lead, and they happily followed him on this journey. As we laughed, drank, and shared stories, the evening went by too quickly. 

But before we knew it, the room was filled with the nights hum and we were all enjoying each others company. It was quite lovely.

I leaned in close to Augustine, whispering, “Hey Augustine, what's up?”

He looked at me with a mischievous wink. “Just enjoying my company!”

“Good answer, Augustine,” I said, chuckling. “Good answer!”

“So what do you think, brother Aquinas?” Augustine turned to Aquinas. “Is God now one of us?”

Aquinas smiled. "It is a mystery, but certainly God’s love and law guide our path."

Augustine nodded. “Exactly! And I would be lost without it. So, let us all raise our glasses to God and to our good fortune in meeting here tonight.”

As we raised our glasses, I couldn't help but think what a wonderful night we were having. And I was so grateful to be able to share it with these great men.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite Queen Elizabeth I of England, Leonardo da Vinci, Nikola Tesla, Alice Waters, and Dora Marquez to my dinner party.

Elizabeth was a formidable monarch as well as a poet and the patron of many of the arts. She was the head of a country and sponsored artists like William Shakespeare, who was instrumental in the English Renaissance. I'd like to hear what she had to say about religion and governance.

Leonardo da Vinci was a scientist, engineer, and inventor well ahead of his time. He designed prototypes of airplanes, helicopters, and tanks centuries before they were actually built. His life and contributions to art and science inspire me to explore new ideas and technologies.

Nikola Tesla was a Serbian-American inventor, electrical engineer and physicist. He is best known for strong work on modern alternating current (AC) electricity, and for his contributions to the development of remote control, radio communications and AC motor. It would be amazing to have a conversation with a person who had so much of an impact on the technology we use today.

Alice Waters is an American chef, restaurateur, author, entrepreneur, and activist who is well-known for her work with organic food, sustainable agriculture, food systems, and food policy. She has been an inspiration to both chefs and activists for over 40 years, and her efforts have had a significant impact on our current conversation about sustainable food systems and environmental stewardship.

Dora Marquez is an award-winning Mexican-American chef and author. She has authored several cookbooks and has won numerous culinary awards. Her work focuses on traditional Mexican and Latino cooking, and she is an advocate for the preservation of these culinary traditions. She was recently the first Latina to receive the Bon Appétit Chef of the Year Award.

It would be amazing to have such a diverse group of people together, and I'd imagine we would have a stimulating conversation about different aspects of our lives, our work, and the world we live in.

answer:
I would choose Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Rumi, and Ray Bradbury for my special dinner party.

Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo were two of the most influential artists during the Italian Renaissance. They understood the human body intimately and used that knowledge in their work. I would be fascinated to talk to them about art and how we understand the human body.

Rumi was a Persian poet who lived in the thirteenth century. He is known for his full, ecstatic spirituality and his writing on the nature of love and our connections to one another. He would bring a rich perspective on the role of poetry in expressing human emotions.

And last, but not least, I would bring Ray Bradbury, the American author and futurist. He took readers to far-off worlds and used his imagination to push the boundaries of storytelling. We could talk about the role of fiction in revealing truths about the human condition and I would ask him about his thoughts on the future of storytelling.

Together, these four figures embody a range of artistic and intellectual endeavors, and I would value their opinions and perspectives on a wide variety of topics.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Dinner Guests:

Galileo Galilei (1564-1642)
Niccolò Machiavelli (1469-1527)
Emily Dickinson (1830-1886)
Stephen Hawking (1942-2018)
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756-1791)

Conversation:

Galileo: *clears throat* We are humbled in the presence of nature. But I wonder, Niccolò. Are our endeavors of natural law truly fitting for a king?

Machiavelli: It is for pragmatism, Galileo. I have written many works on the state, and the nature of political power. Could you speak to me about the nature of ORDER, and the constants and rhythms of the cosmos?

Dickinson: *sticks out hand* Ah, Mr. Machiavelli, it is a pleasure. How do you work in solitude? What secrets do you keep from your subjects? From me?

Hawking: *smirking* I imagine my work in physics has quite a range of practical applications, but that is not in my interest.

Dickinson: Is that not true of love as well?

Galileo: It is in all things, Gregory, whether scientific progress, art or friendship. And that is why I hope we can get through this meal. For without human relationships, there is no purpose in life, philosophy or science.

Machiavelli: If it is any consolation to you, construction of your successor's moving map would have to be imperative, due to your brilliance.

Hawking: *staring blankly* I can see right through that. You want to be placed near the Galileo to catch a few crumbs. No doubt you want to enchant me with your poetry, Miss Dickinson. So you're not an enemy of science, then?

Dickinson: No, of course not. Laws of physics are... quite beautiful in their precision. Perhaps it is why I prefer metaphors.

Galileo: My dear Mrs. Dickinson, is it not an honor to be seated with a true artist, a renowned scientist and a master politician?

Dickinson: *drinks water* All three of you are gentlemen who know the value of beauty in all areas of human experience.

Hawking: Your writings show a keen appreciation of the world and its inhabitants, Miss Dickinson. As do your words.

Dickinson: Thank you, Mr. Hawking. If the conversation invites it, I will share with you some of my thoughts on beauty.

Mozart: On a brighter note, all of you are such wonderful companions. It's very moving and inspiring to witness such a diverse friendship.

Dickinson: Indeed, Wolfgang. I have always been a fan of your compositions. Your work has a delicate balance to it, and I would be honored to discuss this further.

Galileo: What is your strategy, Nikколо? To mesmerize the room with your talent and charm?

Machiavelli: Save the histrionics. If you value your position, you won't cause trouble. And you, Miss Dickinson, should be extra careful. There are many secrets that have yet to come to light.

Dickinson: Niccolò, this is wonderful. Secrets are a very powerful thing, and they are the mother of ethics. Some secrets can be beautiful. Some secrets are necessary for love.

Hawking: *clears throat* I must admit that secrets play a role in my work, too.

Galileo: Mr. Hawking, you say you stay focused on your work, but I can see you're really a people person.

Hawking: *smiling wryly* Galileo, as long as you're here, I could spend forever.

Dickinson: That is a wonderful sentiment Mr. Hawking. *clears throat* Thank you for being with us, all. I believe our conversation and our friendship will continue forever, and we all have a part to play.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Euclid
Copernicus
Galileo
Newton
Einstein

I'd host them in the Maecenas Hall of the Palazzo Pallavicini-Rospigliosi, Rome, Italy, one of the most beautiful rooms in the city. It's bright and has beautiful frescoes by Andrea Appiani, depicting the victories of cardinals.

I would begin by thanking my guests for taking the time to attend this dinner. I would then make a toast to them:

"To Euclid, who said the shortest distance between two points on a plane is a straight line, but his views extend far beyond simple geometry. Euclid, on this one occasion, I hope you'll toss out geometric logic in favor of a few convivial pleasantries. To Copernicus, who turned Copernicus on its head, literally! I understand you did not reveal your heliocentric theory until your last works, as you wished to avoid any possibility of stirring up controversy. Yet during your lifetime, your findings did stir up controversy, nonetheless, and your ideas set the stage for humanity's understanding of the cosmos, despite the church's brutal censorship tactics. To Galileo, you too suffered the same fate as Copernicus, but you were even more outspoken and a much larger threat to the church's authority, so it's a pity you didn't endure much longer past your trial in 1633, but before then, you had the vision to recognize the earth as a planet and not the center of the universe. To John Newton, who succeeded in two of the most important theoretical and experimental sweeps of 17th century science. He constructed a theory of the heavens, uniting Copernican and Keplerian elements into an explanation of planetary motion that relied on a physical force, gravity. Newton, when you aren't busy deciphering the laws of the universe, what are some of your other interests? To Einstein, who Galileo and his predecessors deeply influenced. Your theory of relativity and mass-energy equivalence have lead to technological developments beyond science fiction -- who would think that equations you wrote could translate to gravitational waves and black holes so tangible that we can actually detect them? The works of Isaac Newton and Albert Einstein may be separated by nearly three centuries, but their contributions are woven into the fabric of our understanding of mathematics, physics, and our universe."

We would then enjoy a meal of veal stew and fresh vegetables simmersed in wild boar broth, accompanied by a bottle of red wine from the Chianti region.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The place was set. I took out my chair at a spacious table, and luggage bustled in to arrange four more. 

Muhammad Ali changed into a bright blue-green, double-breasted velvet dinner jacket and sat down. Claude Monet raised his nose and scanned the room in disgust. He turned dismissively to his left and spoke to the painter next to him.

Across the table, I saw Martin Luther King, Jr. was also a slight man, but to his full height with a silk tie pin and carefully starched collar.

Muhammad Ali sat back and raised a glass. "What's going on? I heard we were starting with soup?"

"We are," I said, "Once everyone arrives."

His eyes bulged. "How many times do I have to tell you, Forker, we have guests!"

Claude Monet raised an eyebrow and removed his facial hair. He took out a paintbrush and began to carefully erase his moustache, then plucked out the remaining stubble. Martin Luther King Jr. rolled his eyes and adjusted his glasses. 

Next came Socrate, wearing a red cloth around the middle. He stepped into the light and saw his guests. 

"Ah, Plato!" Socrates shouted energetically, flinging off his garment and striding over with his hands up. "Hey, Aristotle! Crassus! It's so great to see you all!"

He ran his hand through his hair, his eyes sparkling with laughter, as the ancient Greek philosophers squealed with joy and ran toward him.

"Hello, Socrates," I said. "I think everyone's here now. Why don't we start with this..."

"It's Socrates, not Tony," he smiled. "Come on, my friends, let's get down to business!"

He hugged Plato and kissed Aristotle gently on the cheek. 

Claude Monet clutched his precious moustache and was handed a plate of mussels. As he popped them into his mouth, his expression darkened and shook his head. Then he raised one mussels and raised a glass to his friends.

Martin Luther King, Jr. was deep in thought. Finally he looked up, his face alight with enjoyment. "This is truly a great feast. Thank you, Forker, Socrates." 

Monet rolled his eyes. "Only because I made a scene and said I would refuse to go home." 

"Everyone's here," I said. "Let us feast!"

We raised our glasses. "Kalichomas! Kalo sono! Καλη όρεξη!" Socrates shouted, and we sat down to enjoy our meal.
