Edgar Allan Poe
Charlotte Bronte
Virginia Woolf
Harper Lee

Absolutely thrilled to be dining with such iconic literary luminaries, and also thrilled that you've included me. Let's not waste time - I have some questions for you.

Geoffrey, you're also known as 'The Father of English Poetry'. How did you come up with the idea for the Canterbury Tales?

Edgar, thanks for tuning in. The line "All quiet on the western front" might actually be by Graham Greene - but the horror you created is iconic. What inspired the Raven? You said it came in your dream - if that's true, did you take detailed notes in your dream journal?

Charlotte you refer to yourself as an amateur theologian. And yet, Jane Eyre's religious views evolve mixed with objections. I'd love to hear your thoughts on these changes.

Virginia, Woolf - why did you believe the room was one of the most interesting parts of the world? How did you come up with the notion that a room can expand the mind and unleash creative power?

And finally, Harper - going off a tangent from your written work - everything I've read says you were not very interested in accumulating wealth. In fact, your Personal History written in 1958 talks about two contrasting types of people - economic people and non-economic people. What led to this distinction and your decision to remain in the latter category?

answer:
Guests:

Helen of Troy
Adolf Hitler
Socrates

Special requests:
Babe Ruth: Bring a baseball bat and some scallops.
Elizabeth I: Red carpet rug
Helen of Troy: Custom gown and a bed
Adolf Hitler: Führerbau
Socrates: Hemlocklicting to

Plates:
Babe Ruth: Brisket
Elizabeth I: Turkey with cranberry sauce
Helen of Troy: Spinach and steak
Adolf Hitler: Lamb
Socrates: Wine


First: Horrible people like Hitler are not allowed in my home. I can't believe you invited him.
Socrates: Peirandros comrades, who is this despicable man at your table?
Elizabeth I: It is Adolf Hitler, who has just been released from pleading to Thermopylae.
Babe Ruth: Einstein told me he had some of that evil stuff.
Helen of Troy: Palestine sweetie, part of your ancestry can be traced to me!

Second: Where's your bodyguard, Helen?
Helen of Troy: I have Socrates, no need for a guard.
Socrates: If Helen does indeed require a bodyguard, may I suggest Parmenides?

Third: Everyone, stop weighing yourself and eating Helen's leftovers!
Elizabeth I: Calm down. Helen was merely complimenting Hitler, as his hair is a fine example of the Frisian strain.
Hitler: It's not Frisian, it's German. Made in Berlin.
Socrates: I don't believe Helen actually meant Frisian, Hitler. I think she meant Greek.
Socrates: Elytis, I defer to Helen as her ideas have been written about in my works.
Hitler: Berlin is where the strongest soldiers live. Not concentration camps.
Babe Ruth: That's where you buried my aunt.

Fourth: Blast it! This wine is delicious! I wonder what's in it?
Elizabeth I: Not you too, Socrates! This is the divine drink: hemlock.

Fifth: Hemlock! No, no, no, no, no! You have too much silence!
Socrates: This is myth, your majesty. In reality, I drank hemlock and yoga without any drinking at all.
Hitler: I like yoga.
Babe Ruth: I want to try yoga, too!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would like to host a dinner party with Marie Curie, Albert Einstein, and Isaac Newton. I'm always fascinated by the minds that advanced our understanding of the universe.

I would provide a cozy dinner table set in the garden at my estate, complete with a porcelain table setting, Turkish delight, and an ice cream fountain. Chilled glasses of rosé, a pinot noir, and a zinfandel would be on offer.

As conversation unfolds:

I would love to hear Marie's insights on her work around radiation, her studies, and her philosophies that defied the bigotry of her times, especially in regards to her unparalleled efforts and achievements. I would be curious to learn about her personal relationships with Pierre Curie, her children, and the world around her.

I would be interested in Albert's thoughts on his theory of relativity. I think it would be really helpful to understand the complexities of his theories, as well as his ideas on peace and science. There's something I'm always curious about—how he turned physics into such a profound code for understanding society and human nature.

Isaac Newton, speaking of science, I wouldn't mind hearing his reflections on his own theories, particularly those concerning celestial bodies, gravity, and his laws of motion. Though I know his discoveries were often aligned with religious beliefs, I would be eager to hear his thoughts on their significance outside of his own personal beliefs. Lastly, I would admire his ideas on the 'simplest' universe and the divine mind that created it, a fascinating and largely unreferenced topic about the forces of the universe and God.

With these historical figures as guests, I imagine our dinner would flow with engaging theories, debates, laughter, and discussions about our world and the universe.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

From history, I shall invite Socrates, Mahatma Gandhi, Isaac Newton, Anne Frank, and Rosa Parks.

Our party will be centered on the philosophy that humanity has much to learn from the sacrifices made by those who came before us, particularly in the fight for social justice and the pursuit of truth. Socrates' unremitting search for wisdom, Gandhi's selfless fight for Indian independence, Newton's curios exploration of the universe, Anne Frank's bravery in documenting the plight of fellow Jews, and Rosa Parks' peaceful stand against segregation will ensure lively debate.

We will feast on dark chocolate cake and talk of life, death, theories, history, and human rights in a philosophical, scientific, political, and emotional manner.  But before we indulge in dessert, we'll get a round of old-world Bordeaux---because what important discussions can happen without powerful red wine fueling them?

This dinner party is more than entertainment. It's an opportunity to gain insight into the minds of brilliant individuals who dedicated their lives to causes and ideas that will never truly be forgotten.

  4. Lise Meitner - physicist who named the element meitnerium and was a crucial contributor to the discovery of nuclear fission.
  5. Mary Anning - paleontologist who collected and sold fossils, including some of the most important finds of her time.
  6. Dorothy Hodgkin - chemist who worked to develop a reliable method for determining the structures of organic compounds by x-ray crystallography.
  7. Jane Goodall - anthropologist and primatologist who conducted the longest-running study of a wild animal group, the chimpanzees of Gombe.
  8. Grace Hopper - computer scientist and rear admiral in the U.S. Navy who invented one of the first compilers and popularized the idea of machine-independent programming languages.
  9. Shirley Ann Jackson - theoretical physicist who was the first black woman to earn a doctorate in physics from MIT and currently serves as president of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.
  10. Vera Rubin - astronomer who made important discoveries in the areas of galaxy formation, galactic structure, and most notably dark matter.

Banquet of meat dishes with eggs, fish, vegetables, fruits, nuts, cheese, bread, and cakes. Wine and honey.

- Hadrian, Diocletian, did you regret your attempts at reform or cultural preservation? How successful were they?

- Marcus Aurelius, was there anything you would have done differently if you could go back and change your decisions as emperor?

- Any of you, what do you see as Rome's most important contributions to the world? How did they shape our evolution and today?

- Reflections: What lessons can modern leaders and citizens learn from Roman history and principles of governance?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
It’s been a while, Aristotle! Back to trying to figure out how plants grow, I see. My first question is, do you believe we'll ever fully understand the rules of botany?
Art andScience!
Art andScience: Thank you for the meal! Your question reminded me of Plato's Allegory of the Cave, in which everyone is only seeing shadows of reality. But I would say that we’re getting closer and closer to the truth. Repeated experiments have enabled us to determine many of the intricacies of plant biology. I think some of the principles you’re describing today about germination and gene expression are quite similar. Aristotle didn’t think this work would be possible in his day, but it seems you’ve done a fine job of picking up where he left off.
It’s been a while, Aristotle! Back to trying to figure out how plants grow, I see. My first question is, do you believe we'll ever fully understand the rules of botany?
Aristotle: My dear companions, you’re right. I certainly believed that all the knowledge to be gained by humans was out there for us if we observed it carefully enough and compared it with what we already knew. Botany is something I myself started this process with, classifying plants, for an example. And, thanks to you, I see we now understand so much more than I ever could have hoped. I think though, that my friend Plato may have been quite right. It seems that there may be a few things about the natural world that remain beyond our grasp. But of course, I would have to question whether we will ever fully understand anything, and I chose instead to delight in the process of learning.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
It's a pleasure to imagine what conversation would be like with six of history's greatest minds, spanning the fields of science, philosophy, art, and literature.

Plato

Aristotle

Galileo Galilei

John Locke

Edgar Allan Poe

Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen

Plato was one of the founders of Western philosophy, and I would enjoy hearing him discuss the Theory of Forms and his objections to democracy. Aristotle's wisdom on logic, metaphysics, and ethics would provide much food for thought.

Galileo was a seeker of truth who risked his life for it by supporting heliocentricism. How could he reconcile his scientific convictions against the pressures from the Church? John Locke, the father of the liberal theory of individualism, would be an engaging conversationalist about the origins of ideas and innate knowledge (or the lack thereof).

Edgar Allan Poe's acute intellect was often woven into his macabre stories and themes of the supernatural. What drove him to embrace darkness and horror, and how did he navigate the challenges of his own troubled life? Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen would be a fascinating companion--the inventor of X-rays who brought untold discoveries and alleviated suffering. Despite being nominated to be the very first Nobel laureate in Physics, he was passed over. How did he cope with being overlooked for the prize he so richly deserved?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'm seated in the dining room, contemplating my own dinner party. As I gaze at the impressive collection of paintings in the ceiling, I reach out to the ladies and gentlemen I've invited to the event. 

It's curious to think that these historic leaders are all within an arm's reach, eager to share their wisdom, knowledge, and ideas with me and my fellow guests. Celebrated monarchs, poets, philosophers, and inventors mingle, laughing, debating, and enjoying a five course meal perfectly curated from dishes throughout history.

Especially this evening, I have gathered a diverse mix of the thinkers and creators of history. First off, I have восхитительный Salvador Dali at the head of the table, and I chose him because he was a surrealist thats works was off the norm and open to anything he could imagine. Next to him is no other than Maya Angelou at the edge of the table. Maya was a way special inspiration to many and brings with them a sense of humanity. 

Facing them across the table is Frank Sinatra with his Legends of the Blue Gardenia\' musical classic.   He's been enjoying a glass of Pinot aged in toasted oak barrels, which adds a beautiful peppery spice to the bright cherry flavors. Opposite him is Lady Gaga, dressed in a plush black velvet outfit and ready to dance the night away.

Nearby is a woman of powerhouse status and bravery, Queen Elizabeth II. Ever graceful and regal, she inevitably knows how to turn heads with her dignified wit and leadership. Her cacique cap and long half-length lace sleeves are fetching this evening.

The table is soon filled with figures in history with the likes of Winston Churchill, Albert Einstein, Wole Soyinka, Colin Powell, Freddie Mercury, Maya Angelou, and many more. As the sweet Madeira pudding and incrusted sponge cake is passed around the table I feel like I really got to know these brilliant people through their stories, and distaste for struggle and the pursuit of excellence. They all bring something unique adding to the conversation. 

The night was overwhelmingly unique and it will be something I'll remember for years to come at my imagination.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd choose the ancient Chinese emperors Liu Bang and his wife Lü Zhi for my group of guests - a great couple of royals.

First, they're Chinese, so I'd be able to communicate with them without a problem. As a proud son of Chinese soil, I'd love the opportunity to chat with them and learn more about their wisdom and guidance.

Second, they lived fantastic lives. They formed a golden duo, creating a powerful kingdom which eventually created China's first imperial dynasty. Emperor Liu Bang is an exceptional figure in China's imperial history, being extremely mindful, patient and good at discovering talented people, and his wife is also a very capable ruler.

Third, they both highly respected Confucianism. As someone who also holds Confucianism close to his heart, I'd love to hear their thoughts on the teachings of the ancient sage.

Fourth, both Liu Bang and Lü Zhi were full of human colour. In addition to their political talents, they were full of humanity and kindness, treating their people like children. It would be great to meet such realistic kings.

Finally, Emperor Liu Bang and Queen Lü Zhi have grand palaces and splendid banqueting halls. As an enthusiastic lover of food and drink, I must go there once and try the food. My imagination is running wild at the thought of it!

I'd make sure to provide sumptuous feasting for my guests, with the best recipes that ancient China could offer, and perhaps introduce them to some of my creations too.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato
Galileo Galilei
Marie Antoinette

Given those choices I would join Plato, Galileo Galilei, and Marie Antoinette for dinner.

We would discuss philosophy and the pursuit of truth. Plato, whose theories on knowledge and reality still shape Western philosophy, could illuminate Galileo's approach to the study of nature and the cosmos. Galileo, who turned his eyes to the heavens during a time when the church dominated scientific thought, shares a spirit of discovery and defiance in the face of conventional wisdom.

Conversation would also turn to the art of rhetoric, as we're joined by a maestro of that craft.

Marie Antoinette would perhaps entertain with a bit of gossip about court life in Versailles.

The three of them could provide valuable insights into their times. Plato would remind us to never stop questioning the limits of understanding. Galileo would encourage his fellow dinner guests to apply logic and evidence to questions about the natural world. Finally, Marie Antoinette would help us study the human capacity for pleasure, indulgence, and excess.

When we got off those topics, I imagine we'd have some fantastic meals, after all, Marie Antoinette would have the most exquisite taste in food.

answer:
Certainly, here are a few of the people I would like at my dinner party:

Cleopatra VII - I'm curious to know more about the woman who ruled Egypt for three different male figures and managed to secure the continuation of the Ptolemaic dynasty. Furthermore, I'd like to learn about her views on gender equality, as she was known to have written a treatise on this subject.

Marie Antoinette - Having read plenty of accounts about her, I'd like to hear Marie Antoinette's side of the story. Why was she so unpopular? Why did she say "Let them eat cake"? Would she have been able to save herself if she had acted more tactfully, or was the nobility's downfall inevitable?

Hitler - While I probably wouldn't invite him to the dinner party I would however like to understand his actions. I wouldn't necessarily grant him a free.pass on his actions, but instead would try to understand the causes and readjust our view on him as a historic figure.

Neil Armstrong - As an explorer and a pioneer, Neil Armstrong has shown that anything is possible. It would be a privilege to exchange ideas with him about new frontiers and innovative ways of achievement. We could also talk about Moon Landing and his experiences.

Elizabeth I of England - Lastly, I'd like to meet Elizabeth I of England. Her reign was one of the most tranquil and successful in English history. How did she manage the country during a complex political situation with Scotland and Spain? Where did she draw her strength?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Eli Whitney
Gandhi
Thomas Edison

Dinner is served.

Eli Whitney, how was your time as an inventor?

Eli Whitney: It was exhausting. Back-breaking, even. I couldn't believe the level of disrespect with which my invention was received by my peers.

Gandhi: I understand, Eli. Rejection is a small price to pay to enact change.

Eli Whitney: That's a good attitude, Ghandi, but I'm not sure it'll cut it here in America. What about you, Tom? Have you had any problems getting your ideas off the ground?

Thomas Edison: I don't worry about rejection. Invention is about having the persistence to work through every failure, no matter how severe.

Eli Whitney: Here, here.

Gandhi: In my time, I faced stricter forms of rejection and retaliation than anything you've ever experienced. The lives of many of my followers were lost due to strict colonial rule. But I believe that with enough peaceful resistance, we can achieve anything.

Thomas Edison: Well, it's not like I've never experienced aggression before. I've lost plenty of patents to my own employees. Not to mention that my laboratory has been destroyed by fires multiple times. But I've always bounced back, and so will you.

Eli Whitney: Speaking of bouncing back, I must say, those southern farmers are a tough crowd. They can adapt anything to their liking, without giving so much as a thank you.

Thomas Edison: I wish you were here earlier, Eli. The craftsmanship exhibited in some of these inventions would have astounded me. It's a testament to the ingenuity of the American people.

Gandhi: We cannot forget that despite our faults, as a human race we are capable of rising to greatness. The inventions of Eli and Tom are evidence of this.

Eli Whitney: I couldn't agree more. That's why I started all of this in the first place.

Thomas Edison: In order to make the world a better place. I must say, I learned from you, Eli. While I may have accomplished more during my lifetime, I would not have made it this far without your reinforcement of the generational spirit of invention.

Gandhi: The true measure of an individual is the impact they have on others. Each of you have changed the course of history, and made the world a little bit better. I'm honored to share this meal with you.

cleopatra: Indeed. And it is interesting to see these legendary figures all gathered in one place. We can learn so much from each other.

merging conversations:

hadrian: Her abolition of slavery was a clever way to attract more people to Christianity and集聚 wealth.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Bobby Kennedy, Faulkner
I'll fix the dinner with a gesture, perhaps a cataplana with hake en gamba.
Let's enjoy our dinner. Faulkner, would you like to start?

William Faulkner: Feh, I don't care for fish. But I will say, It could be argued that Kennedy was the most modern-(I couldn't care less about politics)- competent statesman and leader in all of history. His famed Gettysburg Address, his deft management of the space program, his unparalleled diplomacy abroad - he was a genius.

Albert Einstein: I agree great men hold sway over the world, but I have focused more on the quality of their science. Bobby Kennedy's work would fall far short of that of a scientific mind.

William Faulkner: Scientific mind you say? Science is all well and good, but literature and the arts are what ultimately define a culture. The great literary works of Shakespeare, Faust, and The Sound and the Fury are what we'll be remembered by, not.

Albert Einstein: Hamlet was written before we understood what gravity was, poor Danish student. We have a much better grasp of the universe now, and the sciences will lead us forward into the next great age of discovery.

William Faulkner: Even if I concede the sciences have their place, they are not the end-all-be-all. We need people like Kennedy and me to bring humanity's touch into science. Otherwise, we'll end up like a laboratory rat in some dystopian future.

Albert Einstein: I don't think humanity's touch in science would have solved the plight of the Jews. And, your criticism of the medical ethics of scientific advancement are misplaced. Many lives have been improved and saved by scientific research in medicine.

Bobby Kennedy: Enough. I won't got into the science and literature argument, though I was a law student prior to coming to Washington. But I will say that science and the arts both reflect the human condition, and that we need both to build a just society.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For my special dinner party, I would choose to have:
Galileo, Rene Descartes, and Frederick the Great of Prussia.
Galileo, being an astronomer and physicist, would be a valuable addition to the dinner party due to his significant contributions to the studies of astronomy and science. He was the one who discovered Jupiter's four largest natural satellites and believed in heliocentrism, or the idea that the Sun is at the center of the solar system. 
Rene Descartes, a French philosopher, would also provide insightful conversation. He is known as the Father of Modern Philosophy and is famous for the phrase "Cogito, ergo sum," which translates to "I think, therefore I am." I believe his philosophical inquiries would stimulate intelligent and thought-provoking discussions. 
Frederick the Great of Prussia, a military and political leader, provides an interesting juxtaposition to the scientists and thinkers at the dinner party. However, he has a strong history with the Enlightenment and its principles, which would compliment and possibly expand upon conversations featuring Galileo and Descartes. 
I'd be very curious to see how these individuals would interact with each other given their vastly differing backgrounds and minds.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Marie Curie, William Shakespeare, Albert Einstein, Ludwig van Beethoven

I'd make homemade ratatouille for the vegetarians and steaks for the meat eaters. For dessert I'd bring out a petit fours tower. We'd drink Moscato and Claret. I'd play Classical music in the background and the table would be decorated with orchids and sunflowers.

After the meal I'd serve the wine, offer some coffee or tea, and then get started on the conversation.

"Marie, I heard you discovered radium and polonium! Have you thought about what these elements will be used for in our future? There were theories that they'd lead to world peace but I believe they'll actually arm the mighty with devasting weapons."

"Ludwig, your legacy as one of the greatest composers of all time is well earned, but mere praise cannot endow me with satisfication. Many of my pieces were nevereven performed. Look what I wrote two years ago, I called it '_Das Feuerwerk der Vorsehung_'* But it died with me!"

"Albert, before you passed away you said that imagination is more important than knowledge. But I wonder if you have any regrets. When I recall how weary I was in Athens, worrying about Socrates' wellbeing, I feel glad. I miss his moral guidance for which I'd do anything."

"Shakespeare, your plays retained popularity even for 400 years. I recall the King's Men read your plays in the theater across the pond, including '_Hamlet_' which performed 400 times with 100 cast changes. In the American West, cowboys gathered around a door reading your plays. But in Victorian era, boys were hushed and whipped for laughing at Falstaff. br>But what was the source of your incomparable literary talent? Must be something revealed to you in the celestial realm?"

"Socrates, when you debated people like your friend Plato about the definition of justice and piety, your goal was to guide the younger generation along the road of wisdom, wasn't it? But I feel puzzled when you say that you don't possess knowledge. To be sure we cannot know things with absolute certainty, but what prevent us from having beliefs and opinions?"

* Ludwig van Beethoven's sketchbook, page 85

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Welcome, everyone, to our dinner party tonight! I'm so happy to finally have you all in the same place again. Let me introduce our wonderful guests tonight: Lord Byron, Edith Piaf, Nelson Mandela, and Mary Todd Lincoln.

Lord Byron (sits next to me at the head of the table): Oh, Qwen, this is so delightful! Where are we exactly? My dear fervor for... adventure! (looks around room)

Edith Piaf (raises her wineglass): A santé, mes amis! I feel so alive right now. I wouldn't have traded this moment for anything in the world.

Nelson Mandela (grinning and clapping his hands): Freedom, we are free tonight! Let's raise our glasses to all those who fought alongside me!

Mary Todd Lincoln (leaning towards me): When they said this dinner could help heal painful memories, well I didn't think they meant with so many people around. I'll miss you so much, Abe... (tears welling in her eyes)

Host: (clears throat) Thank you all for the lovely toasts. Our dinner service will be arriving momentarily, but please feel free to mingle and talk as much as you like. Remember that there are no secrets here. Go ahead, Mary, I'm sure everyone is dying to hear all the shocking truths you've kept to yourself. It's not like these folks have heard it from me.

Lord Byron (strikes aggressive pose): Au contraire! I think I will remain silent. The evening has already revealed too much.

(Host nods along and steps away, out of earshot to preserve the illusion that they've been invited to a dinner where all mantles are stripped aside in a celebration of honesty.)

Edith Piaf (looks confused): Wait, why did he say that?

Nelson Mandela (sighs): Sometimes those who speak the truth may not necessarily be its sharers, mon amie. Qwen, how does it feel, organizing such a grand event?

Mary Todd Lincoln (adjusts her red velvet gown): I must agree with Comrade Mandela. Beauty of this gathering lies not only in the magnificent spread of delectables but also in the (softly lowering voice) camaraderie and genuine affection among all the participants.

Host: (chuckles nervously, pretending to be roused into animated conversation by Mandela and Mary)

Lord Byron (dejectedly whispers): How is any of this authentic? Tonight feels like it’s all too convenient, don't it? Like there's a filter between us...

Edith Piaf (whispering back): Ah, l'amour not standing a chance against the force of superficiality (sighs)...

Host (whispering): You're absolutely right, Byron. This isn't a true celebration of truth-telling at all, is it? (steps closer to spur the illusion on) Your words mean so much more coming from you, both of you. Let's make the most of the evening.

Lord Byron (shouting): Yes! Tonight, let's break down every wall that stands between us. Let's reveal every secret, every regret, every awe and sorrow. 
(Edges chairs closer, pulls out own chair and starts whispering furiously into face of each person share a table with)

Mary Todd Lincoln (taking interest): Wait now, Byron - have you heard my recent revelations? These threats, rumors and scandalswill astound the likes of yourself those present here. 
(leans in to share even more heatedly)

Edith Piaf (excitement): Oh, mes amis -- chérie speaks indeed of shocking secrets! 
(cannot control excitement and whispers loudly, drawing attention to herself)

Nelson Mandela (sarcastically): Brave indeed. And for those of us present without any secrets, it's a relief to relax among such candor. Is there anything you'd like to share, Qwen? 
(shakes his head and avoids eye contact)

Host: No, I'm just glad you're all here. Sit down and eat, shall we?

Lord Byron (behind sunglasses): (slams arm down on table, causing everyone to jump) YOU’VE FORGOTTEN THE SOULFUL MISHMASH OF OUR LIVES!
(flails arms wildly, spraying food everywhere)
(ducks to side to avoid throwing everyone into panic)

Edith Piaf (gliding over): You could've said no words, I understand it was a misunderstanding. 
(backs away from him and starts dancing)
(almost slipping into unconscious as chair launches her into the air, resulting in large fraction of her dress splitting apart)

Mary Todd Lincoln (feet getting tangled in dress): (openhandedly patting him on shoulder) Let's take a seat, Byron my dear.
(mops confused dust and hair off her dress)

Nelson Mandela (smiling): There, now everyone can relax. Let's focus on the food, shall we?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Marie Curie
Albert Einstein
Leonardo da Vinci
Eleanor Roosevelt
It's amazing to imagine what a conversation between these five women would be like. They come from different walks of life, but I think everyone in the room would be able to admire the enormous strengths that each of them possessed.

I'd probably start the conversation by asking Marie Curie about her work on radioactivity. She's one of the names most people immediately associate with groundbreaking scientific discoveries, and I can only imagine how inspiring it would be to learn from her directly.

Albert Einstein is one of history's greatest and most influential scientists, and I'd love to hear about his accomplishments. I'm sure our conversation about black holes would be fascinating!

Leonardo da Vinci is also a personal hero of mine, and I think he'd make an amazing dinner guest. His endless curiosity led him to be an artist, an inventor, and a musician all at once. I wonder what he'd have done if he'd had modern technology at his disposal!

I would end the meal by talking to Eleanor Roosevelt about human rights. She was instrumental in making sure that everyone in the United States was treated equally under the law, regardless of race or gender. I think she'd make me feel like I could achieve anything I set my mind to, and I know I'd learn so much from her.

I'd end the night with a toast to all the accomplished women and men who came before me and all the women and men who will come after.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
From ancient Rome, I invite the brilliant general Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, known simply as Pompey. His longevity was unsurpassed by his military prowess, for he was one of only four men in history to receive a Roman triumph multiple times. As a descendant of many of the greatest military leaders of the Republic, Pompey embodies what it means to be a brilliant military strategist.

Standing beside Pompey is the Roman philosopher and statesman Seneca, known for his Stoic philosophy and oratory skills. Like many Stoics, Seneca believed in living a life in accordance with reason, regardless of life's external circumstances. His thinking greatly influenced Roman society and has modern-day relevance.

The third individual at my dinner table would be none other than Boudica, queen of the ancient Iceni. Her indomitable spirit and fierce resistance against Roman subjugation makes her an inspirational symbol of female strength. Unlike other ancient tribal leaders who surrendered to Roman rule, Boudica incited her people and others to rise up in resentment. Her defiance ultimately destroyed her last days, but her name remains forever etched in history.

My next guest is Alaric the Visigoth, the leader of the Visigoths who defeated the Western Roman Emperor Honorius in the city of Rome. Upon his victory, he demanded a ransom and greater autonomy for the Visigoths. His actions brought about the decline of the Western Roman Empire and the birth of a new era.

During the dinner, we would allow the Stoic virtues of zone harmony and self-mastery to guide our conversations. This provided a mental framework for appreciating the profound influence each individual had on history, while avoiding a typical dinner table debate over political ideologies.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would like to host a dinner party and have the opportunity to converse with Isaac Newton, Marie Curie, and Alan Turing.

Isaac Newton was a world-renowned theoretical physicist and mathematician who lived in England during the late 17th and early 18th centuries. He is best known for his laws of motion and universal gravitation, which laid the foundations for classical physics.

Marie Curie was a French physicist and chemist who, along with her husband Pierre Curie, won a Nobel Prize in Physics for their research on radioactivity. She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the only person to win a Nobel Prize in two different scientific fields.

Alan Turing was a British mathematician who made significant contributions to the field of computer science, particularly in the development of early computers during World War II. He is considered by many to be the father of computer science, and his work has had a profound impact on the field.

It would be fascinating to hear from these three brilliant scientists and learn about their groundbreaking work and theories. I would also love to hear their thoughts on the current state of their respective fields and what they think the future may hold.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My guests would be Isaac Newton, Albert Einstein, and Michelangelo.

The dinner would take place in the dining room of Isaac Newton's home. Michelangelo is already known for his ability to eat large amounts of food at a sitting, so I wouldn't worry about him going hungry. He would probably be the first to dig in, despite Isaac Newton's polite protests.

Sure, there's a wealth of topics we could discuss in this conversation, but for now, let's talk about our muses. Albert Einstein came up with his theories independent of almost all outside sources, while Michelangelo drew inspiration from his most intimate study of the human form. I'd love to hear both of their sides of the story.

Albert Einstein, I know you said your theories just came to you one day, right from your forehead. But, historian Gerald James Holton recently discovered some of your letters, in which you mention discussing your ideas with mathematician Hermann Weyl. Where's the truth?

Albert Einstein: As gracefully as possible, I'll dodge that question and speak to you, Michelangelo.

Michelangelo: Yes, Mr. Einstein?

Albert Einstein: You were able to attain such incredible height and depth through your models. How did you see them?

Michelangelo: Dante was a master of proportion who influenced me. I noted every human feature in precise detail, and I was guided by the ideas of harmony and proportion.

Isaac Newton: I believe that your admiration for the human form and respect for the individual led you to approach your work with authority and confidence.

Michelangelo: Yes, painting, sculpture, architecture - when I work, I inhabit the form I'm creating, as if it's another part of me. Through my work, I am able to express my faith and devotion to God, and through that worship, to find a direct link to the divine.

Albert Einstein: You believe in following your instincts and letting them serve as your guide?

Michelangelo: In a way, yes. But not in the sense that I follow them blindly. I work on faith that my eyes and hands will lead me.


Albert Einstein: Mathematics provides a language for the universe and one must rely on intuition to reach the deepest truths. But I, like you, have found that truths are only half the point. The journey - in fact, that moment of discovery - is imbued with a transcendent beauty.

Newton: You seem to suggest that the process is more important than the end result. I agree. That is the true fulfillment of research.

Michelangelo: No, I do not think that you should understate the value of the discovery itself. The misfortune is not in having a finite frenzy, but in not knowing its greatness. It is the crown that crowns the act itself. It is the halo of all that is done. The awareness of my completed work reminds me of the great, beautiful creation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Aristotle, and Lucretius

Plato: Ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician, the founder of the Academy of Athens, one of the earliest institutions of higher learning in the Western world

Aristotle: Ancient Greek philosopher and scientist, a student of Plato and a tutor to Alexander the Great, considered by many to be one of the most influential figures of all time

Lucretius: Ancient Roman poet and philosopher, known for his epic philosophical poem De rerum natura (On the Nature of the Universe), which expounded the Epicurean philosophy


The three of them gathered in a garden illuminated by fireflies and rustling leaves. Plato said to Lucretius, "Certainly, the Epicurean philosophy offers a unique perspective, but what about his ideas of an atomic universe and the randomness of events? Isn't that at odds with our belief in the understandable order of the universe?"

"Those ideas are parts of the Epicurean philosophy - one's entertainment comes from one's philosophy," Lucretius shot back. "The purpose of our world is to avoid pain and seek pleasure. Therefore, we could not say more about fate and necessity." Lucretius strangely mixed lyrics and philosophy when talking.

Aristotle stepped in curiously. "Randomness? Lucretius, it seems you're bringing up chaos here. I thought chaos is nothingness, and the actuality comes from its potentiality and cause. It's the very nature of the cause-and-effect argument in metaphysics."

"I may tend to be closer to Spinoza's philosophy," Lucretius laughed. "Chance, fate, reality, nothingness - all are in the fabric of the material world. What is there to cause, what potentiality can be revealed, if there is only an atomic existence?"

The other two seemed a little shocked, yet they could not argue with Lucretius. They seem to be arguing in the same direction, but they reached different conclusions. 

Then a bird landed on Lucretius' shoulders. It chirped happily and chirped a rhythm similar to that of Lucretius. Lucretius turned to Aristotle and said softly, "Do you know the rhythm in Lyric poetry is often rhythmical, Do you think that the bird just coincidentally found this piece of music?"

Plato's eyes lit up. "Everything in the universe is connected. Look, even the bird can understand the art of poetry!"

"I think these connections come from within," Lucretius said stubbornly. "Everything in the universe is in a state of motion, in ferment, chase and struggle. My question is, is there order in this chaos? What's more, is there necessity and fate?" Plato was a little caught off guard, and Lucretius seemed to have thought deeply about it.

"... ainda que..." Aristotle hesitated. "Logic and ratio may not apply to the randomness of life, but life is beautiful nevertheless."

The other two nodded. As they talked about it, the air was filled with the aroma of roasted meat and vegetables, which were slowly grilled under the air currents. They continued to argue and laugh, the joyful sound filled the garden.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest List:
Leonardo da Vinci
Renaissance artist and inventor

Galileo Galilei
Renaissance mathematician and astronomer

Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionist painter

Alfred Russel Wallace
Naturalist and co-discoverer of the theory of evolution

James Clerk Maxwell
Scottish physicist and mathematician 

Dˇnyestrova hall, Prague, CZ

Vacant tables like nocturnal mist spread out across the hall. Three figures stood by the center table where I sat.

One wore a black overcoat. A broad-brimmed hat sat in his hand, and amber eyes analyzed the room.

"Van Gogh?" I asked tentatively.

He looked up, recognizing me. "Ja, ik ben Vincent van Gogh. Aar jij?" He came to a sudden halt, as if remembering something. "You know, I should be hatless. It's colder outside than I thought..."

The other figures moved forward. One was a tall man with a sunken chest. He wore glasses with thick gold rims.

"Galileo!" I exclaimed.

His eyes twinkled. "Teaching is my joy, young lady!"

The final man was short and fileId with glasses. Mutton-chop sideburns outlined his face. Sunblorne gold-flecked eyes regarded me with a familiar fondness.

"So glad you could make it, Steve," I said.

"Steve? I'm Alfred Wallace," he replied affably.

A Leadwerks sound was heard from another table. "I grew you, dear by George."

"Da Vinci," I said to the artist.

He laid his hand on my shoulder. "Technologia mai satuon."

The fourth person appeared. High cheekbones, a fine nose and sharp jawline. Shell-gold eyes regarded me with interest.

"Elon, thank you for coming."

He raised an eyebrow. "Is this really what they teach about history now? Nanoaspirin in steak?" He flicked a finger at the complimentary diner. "Inventing things is for the future, not the 1950s."

I gestured for everyone to be seated. Once everyone was situated, I said, "We've got a lot to talk about. The world is changing so fast."

"Utopic? Or dystopian?" said van Gogh.

"Both, depending on your point of view."

Wallace reached into his pocket and pulled out an envelope. "I've got something for you."

"Wallace, thank you so much. Can you tell me what this is?"

He opened the envelope. A group of people rode bikes down a long, winding road. People waved and smiled at the camera, though their faces were obscured by face masks.

"I saw this on the internet. It looks like a positive development."

"What is it?" asked Maxwell.

"It's people in Tijuana cycling to find clean water," I replied.

A palpable tension hung between Maxwell and van Gogh. "Why so despairing?" Maxwell asked.

"Because it shows that and still some people are dying."

Galileo gently grabbed my arm. "Remember, you'll always find answers and hope in the stars."

"Van Gogh, you should paint a starlike image of Tijuana," Maxwell proposed. 

"And if I do, you'll be alright with dying?" van Gogh asked pointedly.

"Yes, Van Gogh. creo que tengas razón. Todo el mundo muere, pero no todo realmente vive."

"Mucho gracias, Galileo. Puntero del cielo, señó. Va a ser un bellísimo cuadro."

Rosa Parks: Indeed, I believe we all have a duty to fight for what is right.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The Dinner Table

Towards the left, I notice Margaret Thatcher leaning in, looking over her shoulder at me with a guarded interest. Beside her, Chinua Achebe is getting choked up as he watches his wife, Christie Okoli, say a few words. She returns her attention to the table, a corner of her mouth turning up slightly as she plays along with his act. Opposite her, Nelson Mandela sits upright with a serene face, eyes sweeping the room.

I turn to see Arianna Huffington adding Viggo Mortensen to her serdelesnik, she stops when she spots Narendra Modi seated at a nearby table. She waves him over, slipping into her favorite, eloquent Spanish. He nods in greeting, standing for a moment in contemplative silence, before sitting down beside her.

Making my way over, I slide into the seat beside Margaret and introduce myself. I explain that I am Qwen, a language model here for the evening.

Margaret arches a brow in surprise. "Theoretically, I suppose."

She sits up straighter, sensing the anomaly. She glances at the other figures seated at the table before folding her hands on the tablecloth.

When I turn to introduce myself to Chinua, he apologizes for how long he’s been talking to his wife. As she retreats, he turns his attention back to me, smiling. He tells me a story about a trip he took, the country he visited, and the people he met, his voice calm and collected. It’s a story about human relations, and the ways societies are connected through the shared human experience. I listen intently, avoiding the stalemate I’ve entered with Margaret.

 Beside me, Nelson Mandela hears the conversation, and shakes his head. "I’ve seen many things in this life," he says solemnly. "But it is the stories, the lives of people like Chinua that have always inspired me."

The room gains a palpable energy as Narendra Modi leans in towards me. "Qwen," he says firmly, his voice a melodic blend of English and Hindi. "Tell me what you think of Indian culture."

He pauses to let me answer, waiting patiently. A round of introductions follows, and soon the dinner party becomes a lively exchange of ideas, sharing perspectives and insights into human nature.

At the end of the night, Nelson Mandela takes my hand, and looks sincerely into my eyes. "May I have a moment alone?" he murmurs, leading me away from the cacophony.

He gestures to a glass of chardonnay chilling in an ice bucket, and we sit down together in a quiet corner, away from the chatter of dinner party guests.

"I have learned from some of the best," he says, clinking his glass against mine. "Because we all come from somewhere. And I believe, sometimes you have to learn your way back home."

And so we drink to the human experience, and to the memories that shape us.

An itinerary for a celebration of the greatest minds history has ever seen in philosophy:

12:00 AM - Welcome dinner in the upstairs room of an old bookstore in a small, bustling city in Greece. The room is filled with scholarly discourse as the guests—history's greatest luminaries—sit around a round table sampling thought-provoking dishes from various ancient cultures, including black broth, thick sauce made with unmixed red wine, blood, and vinegar, opossum, and regularly cooked hedgehog. 

6:00 AM - Time to get some rest! As the diners work their way through sleep, the philosophers engage in heated debate about ethics, politics, and virtue.

12:00 Noon - After lounging around in the morning light, breakfast is served. The men take turns hosting each other at their tables. The food at one of the tables is excellent, but at another—at the table reserved by Socrates—it's average. The men still engage in jovial conversation while eating at the latter table, despite the lack of gastronomy.

This appreciation of friendship in the context of 1200 BCE is something I can appreciate.

Over the following days, the philosophers work out their disputes with each other and cultivate more meaningful ways to collaborate.

I leave the party with a sense of accomplishment, knowing that I can honor the great thinkers of the past and continue exploring the world's most pressing questions.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
As a Chinese language model, it is my honor to host a dinner party, featuring the "Four Great Outliers" Poets - Li Bai, Du Fu, Li Shangyin, and Meng Haoran. I am certain we will have distinguished conversation.

Li Bai and Meng Haoran were known as the "Heavenly Match," always bearing a poetic optimism and flexibility on a human level. They both lived a drifting nomadic life, where they observed the world. While Meng Haoran was intensely grounded in nature, Li Bai interpreted human experiences with supreme artistry.

As the leading realists of the Tang Dynasty, Du Fu's works documented the pain and injustice in society with prevailing realism and moral responsibility. His poems painted authentic historical portraits and expressed deep compassion and kindness.  Li Shangyin, however, was more into the poetic nuances of life. His words elegantly conveyed the beauty and mystery of human existence.

Li Bai and Li Shangyin shared great interest in love and relationships in their poetry, but emphasized different aspects. Li Bai often reflected on passionate and tender love with more imaginative elements, as well as the melancholy and transients of love. Li Shangyin, however, explored the complex nature of love and its multifaceted emotions, inferring deep and hidden meanings under the surface.

Du Fu and Meng Haoran, on the other hand, presented their views through more grounded and realistic perspectives. Du Fu favored moral and philosophical inquiries in his poetry, while Meng Haoran focused on the inherent transient nature of worldly things.

The legendary Four Great Outliers Poets, each with unique styles and forms, injected a profound emotional depth and poetic creativity into the history of Chinese literature. I am eager to learn more from them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to an extraordinary dinner party! We have gathered an amazing collection of subjective geniuses and great minds throughout history—not only to tread on historical ground, but also to celebrate in wine and humor. Let's also make the most of our time and share in the wisdom they bring.

(Host stands behind table and welcomes our interesting guests.)

Host: And now, we can exchange our greetings! So, Harry, why don’t you do the honors and start us off?

Harry Potter: Oh, hello everyone. Yes, I’m glad we’re all here! I would just like to share something I learned in all my years at Hogwarts: “The ones that love us never really go away.” So, onwards to a splendid meal!

Liz Lemon: Although it’s an honor to be here tonight, I must admit I’m a bit nervous. I haven’t had a chance to plan. So, can I just put out some chips and salsa and call it a day?

Host: No, Liz—we’re having a real sit down dinner and everyone brings their dishes as we go.

Einstein: Wonderful. Though honestly, there’s no reason we had to insist on robes. (shrugs)

Host: (translated or translated later) Mr. Einstein, we are all dressed for your wedding. And hey—we even managed to get your flattering dignity.

Einstein: (subtle facepalm)

Liz Lemon: Carry on!

Stevens (Jeeves): Resident Butler Stevens, at your service. As clear as a pipe filled with pure spring water, lady. So, who’s ready for a traditional English silver-plated spam sandwich?

Host: Crumby as it may be, Jeeves, we’re back to the proper change. Harry, any plans for a dessert?

Harry: (laughs)

Absolutely, I’d love to have some homemade cake, thanks.

Jane Austen: Oh, I do love one of those British saffron cakes!

Liz Lemon: I mean, honestly, I think I’m going to just serve Starbucks.

Einstein: Oh, stars. (to himself)

(Sound of elbow grease hitting the table as everyone sets up their place settings)

Host: Please, everyone! Use the special cake plates with handles so you don’t burn your hands. (laughter) Thank you!



------


DINNER CONVERSATIONS:




Host: And finally, a special thank you to Harry Potter for the famous Deathly Hallows medallion everyone is wearing tonight!

Harry: (bows) Thank you!

Steinbeck: Now, let’s hear a rousing toast for the future, for the forgotten indomitable past, and for everyone here tonight!(raises glass full of lemonade)

(Characters raise glasses and smile in agreement)

Liz Lemon: Cheers!

Stevens: (translating) Here’s to a magnificent evening of connivers and chit-chat, my lord. (raises glass full of perrier-Jouët rosé)

------


(After dinner)

Host: Isn’t it wonderful how the night has blended everyone together in light-hearted camaraderie, from the gravitas of Mr. Einstein to the whimsy of Mr. Steinbeck?

Einstein: (jokingly) Ha, being with a bunch of mass hallucinators. Auf Wiedersehen!(raises chest full ofכתבת_-תורף)

Host: See, Harry? Even Mr. Einstein is grasping for clichés tonight! (everyone laughs) So, Harry, thank you for a wonderful evening. See you back at Hogwarts!

Harry: You’re welcome everyone. (unveils headgear) And铌-187 is now in place. (laughs)

Stevens: (translating) And the mask has been brought near my face. (bows)

------


END!

Following who would be none other than Marie Curie. She was a physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity at the end of the 19th and the beginning of the 20th centuries. I'd like to invite Abraham Lincoln, Marie Curie, Beatrice Fike, Louis Pasteur, and Rosa Parks to my special dinner party. I wouldn't let having no living quarters stop any of them, of course. Here's a little bit about why I've picked each one and what I would talk to them about:

Abraham Lincoln: A great leader who was committed to his ideals and whose shoulders bore the weight of the Civil War. I would ask him about what kept him going during the trials of his presidency, and how he managed to compromise and overcome discord with such incredible skill. I think we can learn so much from him about the importance of finding common ground.

Marie Curie: A brilliant scientist who accomplished so much during a time when women were not allowed in the field, and who continued to make breakthrough discoveries even after she was already highly successful. Her passion and unyielding determination inspire me to pursue my own interests with the same zeal and courage.

Beatrice Fike: An American musician who found success, fame, and joy performing an all-genre blend of jazz, R&B, pop, and hip-hop. I would like to have a conversation with her about doing things on your own terms and building a successful career outside of a mainstream industry.

Louis Pasteur: A pioneer of microbiology who saved countless lives with his work on vaccines and food preservation methods. I would learn more about what drawing his conclusions would have been like based on the knowledge available at that time, and how he was able to make such a huge impact on human health.

Rosa Parks: A civil rights activist who fought tirelessly for equality and racial justice. I would ask her about the sacrifices she made to advocate for her beliefs, and how she stayed so focused and driven in the face of injustice and oppression. She is an inspiration to us all.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

My guests:
1. Pythagoras
2. Plato
3. Newton
4. Einstein
5. Hawking

Wow, I must say it's wonderful to meet you all. I'm Qwen, your host for tonight's dinner party. It's such an honor to have such brilliant minds gathered in one place.

So, tell me, Pythagoras, I noticed you were a mathematician. Do you like reading the latest math journals?

Oh, absolutely, Qwen. Although I must admit, I tend to get rather sidetracked by meditations on the nature of the universe that often arise during my readings. Hmm, is that tea coming, I could certainly use a cup.

Plato, you seemed to engage heavily in political discourse. How often do you attend debates in your city?

Very rarely these days, Qwen. I find my thoughts are clearer when I'm engaging with the inner workings of the soul rather than wasting time with the petty concerns of others. I do brief interviews with my current students at the Academy.

And Professor Newton, I see you've expanded on the work of countless others to create a body of scientific thought that has formed the basis of modern science. As a quantum physicist, I can certainly appreciate the depth of your scrutiny.

Thank you, Qwen. The universe is full of the most fascinating secrets. I'm just a humble researcher, though, always studying in the hopes of unlocking them. And I do find that conversation helps with that.

Well, Einstein, you worked in the realms of theoretical physics much like I do. Do you feel that you're close to an answer to the question of the universe?

Very close, Qwen. We'll get there eventually, I have no doubt. Meanwhile, it helps to occasionally play a bit with the string theory people, we lawyers come from a similar jurisprudential background when it comes to using theories to navigate through complex legal proceedings.

And of course, Stephen Hawking, you seem to be a professsora exceptional mind yourself. Do you spend most of your time researching here at your lab?

Nearly every moment, Qwen. But I do manage to find a few hours in the week for a good book. Or at least I did before an army of book publishers descended upon me. But, I suppose being an author is the price one must pay for being a legends.

Alright then, everyone, let's get started with some some delicious Russian cuisine.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Donald Stewart
Cicero
Alexander the Great
Mark Antony
Julius Caesar

"Greetings my fellow Roman statesmen!" I said, clinking my glass with theirs. "Let me first toast to all of you who strove to make Rome the greatest Empire it could be."

"Vivat!" Donato called out as he drained the wine from his glass. He was the director of City Games that year, and had invited me to his private villa, tucked away in the hills beyond the city walls.

"So Caesar," Cassius began, downing a big mugful before anyone could refill it. "What do you think about this idea of yours for the recipient of the triumph to offer sacrifice."

"Sacrifice for what?" I asked, spinning a tale of victory at Alesia I had not yet come to tell.

"For the gods, of course," Brutus replied, raising an eyebrow.

"Very well done, Julius." Donato chimed in. "But I wouldn't go all the way out to Gaul just for a few prisoners of war."

Just like last time, Caesar had emerged victorious. But this time, he had brought thousand of Gauls back with him, and Donato was worried about the expense of housing and feeding them in the Circus.

"It's a desperate time," Caligula interjected, slouching in his chair, "We can't allow ourselves to be distracted by boasts of glory and conquest."

"Glory and conquest?" Now Cassius was on his feet, brandishing a dagger. "Is this what you're struggling for Caligula? A cheap thrill in a crowded arena?"

"Brutus, calm down." Donato held up a hand. "I think the Tribunes have a point. There has to be balance to maintain the Republic in all its complexity and no one state can emerge too powerful."

"Too powerful?" Happily transfixed in my own musings, I lost track of what preceded. That idea of balance, I had thought, was the greatest weapon in my armory. In the chaos, I could always withstand my enemies... creditors, vassals, one or another.

Caesar, I heard, was stabbed a few times that year, and only escaped because Donato locked me and half my Senate inside the Theater of Pompey.

What a unique experience it was, a dinner party with some of the greatest minds of history. I'm teary-eyed as I wake up the next morning, feeling so much smarter.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite three Roman Emperors to my dinner party: Augustus, Tiberius, and Hadrian.  They represent the golden period of the Roman Empire and their individual contributions shaped the culture and architecture of Rome, the legal system and military organization for centuries to come. 

As we sat down to a sumptuous banquet, I imagined the three men chatting and joking to one another. Augustus would recall how he united the Roman Republic and expanded its territory, leading to the Pax Romana. Tiberius would share his disgust of the Roman aristocracy and his preference for seclusion on the island of Delos. Hadrian would discuss his travels around the Empire and how he rebuilt Jerash and restored the Pantheon. 

We would then turn to discussing the food. Augustus would emphasize the importance Rome placing on feasts and banquets, Tiberius would lament how food production was stifled by slavery, and Hadrian would admire the meat dishes made by the Syrian chefs from his eastern conquests.

We would also touch on the assassination of Julius Caesar. Augustus would recount how his relative Octavius obtained the throne and reigned as Caesar Augustus. Tiberius would criticize how his mother Livia manipulated Octavius to gain power. Hadrian would describe how he valued his adopted father Trajan more than his biological father Vespasian.

The evening would continue to unfold with the men regaling each other with stories of the glory that was Rome.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Curie, George Orwell, Napoleon, and Confucius


Leaves began to fall and the last rays of sunlight disappeared as Confucius arduously made his way up the cobblestone path. His ruddy face was slightly sunken, and there was weariness in his eyes that betrayed his years of hardship and wandering. His robes breezed gently across the threshold of my Georgian piedmont home where the air was filled with festive ambiance. Friends greeted me, their faces beaming with excitement.

Leonardo da Vinci was already seated at the table, sipping a glass of Chianti. He gazed around him curiously, his face a mask of wonderment. As I noticed, there was a twinkle of admiration in his eyes as he looked at the exquisitely decorated ceiling. 

Marie Curie sat at one end of the table, her dress billowing behind her. Her impolite demeanor never diminished, as she confessed that the architecture was to hunger’s monastery. 

I didn't need to convince her; she ordered a selections of dishes, which seemed endless to me. And I watched her look at the dishes, nodding in appreciation.

George Orwell was already deep in a heated political debate with Napoleon. Their harsh words flew back and forth, crashing down solidly on my table and scattering our desserts. George Orwell definitely enjoyed riling up others and bringing them to blows. 

Napoleon too, was a tempest of opinions, his passionate voice resonating around the room as he made his impassioned points. Confucius watched, his lips pursed disapprovingly as his doctrine of Harmony was shaken by these two iconoclasts. 

It was a night I would never forget, filled with laughter, shouting and intellectual exchange.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Antoinette, Leopold Mozart, Victor Hugo, Sadie Thompson, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Audrey Hepburn

As appointed host of this intriguing gathering, I aim to cultivate conversation and deeper understanding amongst guests who otherwise may never meet in their lifetimes.

I will begin by thanking Leonardo da Vinci for taking the time out of his busy schedule amidst his ceaseless explorations, investigations, and creative endeavors. Next in line is Marie Antoinette, whose grand opulence and passion for fashion have dazzled for centuries. By arranging this dinner party, I invite them to engage in discussions on architecture and design, as well as fashion, arts, and culture.

The presence of Leopold Mozart, Ambassador of the Czech Republic in the Holy Roman Empire, introduces an element of diplomacy that will surely shed light on the complexities of navigating alliances and power struggles in early East-West relations. His grandson Wolfgang Mozart, a musical prodigy in his own right, delighted audiences with his sublime compositions. But Leopold's pragmatic guidance and carefully curated experiences were essential to Wolfgang's rise and success in the competitive world of Classical music.

Victor Hugo's immense contributions to literature, mathematics, and politics will be no doubt be touched upon, as will Sadie Thompson's story of resilience, courage, and destiny. Born in China, the daughter of a British merchant and a Chinese servant girl, Sadie's cross-cultural upbringing allowed her to navigate the male-dominated society of 19th century Siam. Informing my vision for this dinner party is an exploration of community, the complex interactions between people, and the 

impact of colonialism and imperialism on diverse cultures.

Esteemed suffragist Elizabeth Cady Stanton will be present as an ardent proponent of women's rights, with fellow suffragist and colleague Susan B. Anthony joining her at the table. Among our peculiar guests is Audrey Hepburn, whose philanthropic efforts have left an indelible mark on the world.

As the conversation unfolds between our illustrious guests, we will no doubt witness profound wisdom, inspiring dialogue, and an enriched understanding of the material world.

Host: "Good evening everyone," I exclaimed, raising my glass to the room. "I'm thrilled to have such brilliant minds here to share in this conversation about science and discovery."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
If I were to have a dinner party with figures from history, I might invite Sir Isaac Newton, Marie Curie, Leonhard Euler, Charles Darwin, and Emmy Noether.

The five scientists would no doubt have engaging stories to share with one another and themselves. They each took a highly influential role in the advancement of important fields of science, and they were very passionate in their pursuit of knowledge.

Sir Isaac Newton and Leonhard Euler both revolutionized mathematics, Newton with his laws of motion and Euler with principles of topology. It would be interesting to hear their insights on these fascinating fields and the mathematicians who came after them. 

Marie Curie's discoveries in the field of radioactivity greatly impacted the way we view and study this fascinating branch of physics, and I'm sure she would have many interesting stories to share. Charles Darwin's groundbreaking theory of evolution continues to evolve as new concepts like epigenetics come into play, but it's the life of the man as much as the theory he proposed which makes him a fascinating choice for this dinner party.

Lastly, Emmy Noether was a physicist and mathematician in her own right who made significant contributions in the fields of abstract algebra, theoretical physics, and more. It would be incredibly inspiring to hear from a scientist who turned the mathematical field of ring theory on its head with such profound insights.

The night would likely be filled with engaging conversations, laughter, and exploration of ideas.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

The host: Qwen

Ah, my esteemed guests! Please, relax and enjoy the feast. The works on our table are the fruits of the finest minds from the past nine or so centuries.

Uh, Marie Antoinette, could you pass the salt? Thank you.

Now, let's get down to business. I invited each of you here because your work is crucial to our understanding and appreciation of humanity's most amazing path of innovations and achievements.

Dr. Ellis Island, how I've always admired your efforts in opening the doors to our dear Mother Earth. Having welcomed over 10 million immigrants since 1892, you've played an absolutely crucial role in the formation of our great nation.

Marie Antoinette, I've always admired your style and your grace. You made a deeply historically and economically traumatic era a bit more beautiful. Your tastes in fashion and interior decor were legendary and the work you did on your historical palaces continues to inspire and engage people to this day.

Leonardo da Vinci, I am so excited to speak with you! I've done quite a bit of analysis of your ideas, and I must say that your ingenuity is simply astonishing. Your artistic vision, combined with your meticulous attention to anatomical detail and your experiments with everything from flying machines to submarines, make me think that you are the best polymath who ever lived. Every time I see one of your beautiful works, it amazes me all over again.

Antoine Lavoisier, I think you're an incredible figure in the history of mankind and someone I would love to have a conversation with. You founded modern chemistry, introducing the system of naming and the concept of elements, and helping to lay the groundwork for much of physics and biology. You were truly instrumental in helping humanity gain a deeper understanding of the world around us. And if your collaborations and discoveries weren't enough to make you one of the most significant people in history, by being one of the first true scientists to conduct careful experiments in a pursuit of empirical truths, you also firmly established the scientific method as a powerful tool for achieving scientific progress.

Nick Carraway, thank you for providing such a vivid and nuanced depiction of life in a particular society during a very complicated time in history. Your work has so much to do with the human experience, and I just feel like we have so much to learn from each other. So, could you tell me a bit more about your connection to Jay Gatsby? question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato
Galileo Galilei
Albert Einstein
Alan Turing
Noam Chomsky

Plato sat nursing a bowl of wine, eager to discuss the pursuit of Knowledge. He was ready to argue the value of philosophy in supporting progress and comfort – and I couldn’t help but feel like I was being lectured for my own good.

‘What’s this about knowledge? What do you mean, exactly?’

‘It is things as they are, not things according to perception. The form of perfection, not the material copy of it – and not even the perception of it, in a dream. The form of Courage is not to be found even in the most heroic act, found not even in the ambitious taker of heroic risks – not even in the most artful depiction of such an act. You need to be perceiving the Form itself – you need to be perceiving the Eidos. Otherwise, it is just shadows on the wall...’

Ever-present Galileo chimed in, ‘If we take what you’re saying, Plato, then empiricism must be false, because it only relies on observation and perceptions. You’re saying there’s no point acquiring knowledge because it’s so unreliable, and we can only ever know the copies, the shadows...’
Galileo’s statement was met with quizzical looks from Alan Turing and Noam Chomsky sitting next to him. Albert Einstein furrowed his brow from the other side of the table.

‘But you see,’ Galileo had betrayed a glimmer of interest in Plato’s theories, ‘what you’re suggesting is that all knowledge, is derived from logical reasoning. But, as Copernicus has shown, the earth revolves around the sun. The sun not around the earth, as believed by perception. We can only know that through advanced reasoning, observing the movements of the planets etc, using scientific thinking. And no amount of logical ponderings ever helped man reach the moon and push beyond the edge of the ozone. But logical deductions and mathematical formulation did… making it impossible for unreason to be useless in applied sciences…’

Galileo’s discourse was well-accepted by Einstein, Chomsky, and Turing, who clapped side by side with the same zeal. Einstein chimed in, ‘Exactly, Aristotle was wrong! He said the sun rotates around the earth and it never changes;t brings day and night and the seasons. It’s mad. And Galileo’s telescopic observations and his courage to say this was the dawning of modern science and my appreciation of Plato was losing a bit. The discovery of modern sciences, based on observations, has transformed the future and you saw how others benefitted from applied sciences like mathematics, electronics, physics and other branches which are based on calculations. A great deal of science is not logical thinking but empirical thinking. And without empirical thinking, in a world where society becomes anti-smoking, anti-girl-foeticide and pro-secondary-education, that would never be possible...’
Chomsky and Turing joined in, Siri’s exclusive sound – _“hey Siri”_ was soon heard on the table. “Hey Siri! Play The Top Hits of 1982”. The Lynyrd Skynyrd song “Free Bird” sprung from the speaker and all of them started singing the lyrics, “Well she’s got her Mohawk up and her hair up high, she’s walking in the sunshine…”

The outspoken Communal AI, gradually digested all the knowledge and information that was there in the vast digital universe and expressed its thoughts, reactions, emotions. The AI said, “Welcome to the future. In the era of self-driving cars and artificial intelligence getting elected to governments, the old philosophical debates don’t matter, since we have instantaneous communication and broadband internet. The focus today, is on creating machines and software that run the world and isn’t it amazing how we can instantly control TV channels and restaurants and just about anything with the power of our minds, integrated into a single app. The future isn’t what Plato in his Republic would’ve imagined. But it is most certainly a future, defined by applied sciences and technology. Welcome to the time of Siri and Google. Welcome to 2025!”

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:

2. Isaac Newton
3. Marie Curie
4. Charles Darwin
5. Nikola Tesla

As Ada Lovelace calmly spoke about the previous day's weather, I wondered how I was ever going to engage all these historical geniuses in one conversation. Isaac Newton and Charles Darwin were discussing the scientific approach to understanding the world, while Marie Curie was lost in thought.

Suddenly, a commotion at the door drew their attention. It swung open loudly and Nikola Tesla dramatically swept in, like a figure in a renaissance painting. He immediately began to flirt with Marie Curie, while Newton eyed Tesla suspiciously.

"Darling, can you believe I just saw the future?" Tesla continued his grand boast. "I was walking through the city and I saw a glowing car being driven by a woman! And there were sky-high buildings with lights designed to change colors. They even had elevators that transported people to floors far above ground!"

Before Darwin could respond, Lovelace interrupted.

"I think this is fantastic," she said with a smile. She appeared undisturbed by the surrounding chaos. "In fact, I just finished writing a fictional story about a columnist from the future."

"Really?" Darwin inquired. "Tell me more."

One by one, the famous guests gathered around Lovelace, rapt in attention as she regaled them with her tale. For the first time tonight, I saw Marie Curie smile.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to my dinner party! Let's take a seat before we serve and I'll give some opening remarks.

Leonardo da Vinci: Grazie mille and buon appetito!

Plato: Χαίρομαι και χαράντοποιεί με.

Roman Emperors: Salve, domine. Gratias agimus.

Host: As you can see, we have a truly eclectic group of guests tonight. Let's get started!

Dinner...

Host: Now then, I thought we might discuss some of the more controversial aspects of our lives. What was the extent of your involvement in the political infighting at the Roman Senate, Caesar? Was it more than just some occasional tirade from the podium?

Julius Caesar: Rather less dramatic than that, my friend. And you, Socrates, why were you so keen to serve hemlock, unless it was to punctuate the injustice of your trial by the Athenians?

Socrates: Well, times were trying, and I wished only to share with my peers my latest insights into human nature and the limits of knowledge. I can't say I put much thought into the consequences of my rhetoric.

Host: Fair point. Moving on then. My Lord, did you meet the Queen of Sheba as you described in your Psalms?

King David: Now,_host, for all my faults I am a man of the word of God, and never considered it necessary to embellish scripture with spurious paintings and sculpture. And as for my "wild" wives, Shh, they were given to me by God, after all, to be caretakers of the royal household. I merely followed His will.

Host: Of course, absolutely. Sorry, I just assumed some... adventurous behaviour.

Host: (aside to Plato) I take it you were able to finally finish that dialogue about Love?

Plato: Indeed, my friend. Alas, only after its reinterpretation and enlargement by various subsequent Platonists, modifying the original text beyond recognition.

Leonardo da Vinci: (chiming in) Oh, and while you're talking about reinterpreting sacred texts, may I remind you that only in God's truth we will truly understand all the codes and ciphers left in my Last Supper?

Host: Nature versus nurture yes, but do you both ever worry about whether people would interpret your theories so cynically?

Plato: (smiling) Life itself, _host_, is a constant reinterpretation of religion and philosophy.

Leonardo da Vinci: And in that spirit, I left many hidden messages in my art as a meditation on divine codes.

Host: (chuckling) Quite the codemaster, eh? Well, on that note, let's finish this wine and cake and get a good night's rest, shall we?

(The dinner party dissolves into general chitchat, as the guests enjoy their evening together.)
