question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Reynard the Fox
Calvin Coolidge
Henry David Thoreau
Whittling Away in the Woods
Good evening, gentlemen. I trust everyone is enjoying their wine and appetizers. So, let's get right to it. Reynard, I heard you were quite the scissors operator in your time.
Calvin Coolidge: I believe gentlemen prefer the term foxhole admirer. Reynard the Fox:
Ah, I see. Well, Coolidge, your monument will be erected shortly.
Calvin Coolidge: Indeed it will.
Henry David Thoreau: I find the debate surrounding Foxhole Admiration rather... dissolute. Is it not better to examine how these men's deeds, not their reputations, impact society?
Calvin Coolidge: True enough, Henry. Though I must admit, knowing the good names of those who are patrons and supporters can be beneficial in the halls of power.
Reynard the Fox: Speaking of power, it's something I find quite... fascinating, shall we say. I suppose our esteemed Mr. Coolidge can speak to its seductive nature.
Henry David Thoreau: Reflecting on power from the perspective of those who embody it, as well as those who are its subjects, reveals the underlying truths of society.
Calvin Coolidge: Indeed, power is a tricky thing. It must be wielded with care.
Reynard the Fox: Always a smart one, aren't you, Coolidge? Henry David Thoreau: I must take issue with the phrase "Foxhole Admiration." The true admiration lies in the respect one has for the work and (or) actions of all individuals, not just those in positions of power.
Calvin Coolidge: Meditation on power and its proper role in society is a worthy pursuit, Thoreau.
Reynard the Fox: I agree. Power is something to be respected and admired, but it's also something that can be exploited. And in the end, it's up to us to decide how we want to be remembered.

I select a corner next to the windows, taking in the splendid views afforded by the expansive building. My guests are seated on the other side, making conversation with one another and ignoring me altogether.

Determined to soak up the wisdom of these great luminaries, I boldly join their discourse. We spend half an hour on esoteric subjects, then move on to more practical matters, eating and drinking heavily throughout our discussion.

At my request, we start our "real" party after dinner. We take turns recounting the most interesting adventure each of us has embarked on during our lifetimes. Despite our great achievements, Pythagoras was disappointed he never got the chance to visit Asia Minor, and had hopes of doing so for years. Marie Curie spoke at length about her adventures dealing with uranium, and of the energy she felt at the prospect of developing x-rays. Euler indulged in tales of trying to end run physicians by prescribing himself large meals. Aristotle engaged us with stories of sparrows and cranes in North Africa.

As the night wore on, they grew even more chatty. Thanks to the casual, low-pressure embarrassed feelings that persisted through all of it, I had a chance to ask specific questions about their discoveries, their lives, and their boys' club-like world of 4th century Italy.

I learned that Pythagoras was something of a naturalist. Aristotle was often given the credit for making a scientific study of plants and animals, but Pythagoras may have beat him to it. He and his followers conducted experiments on them. On crustaceans and mollusks, they ran all sorts of tests.

After the Curie's discovery, Marie had a assistants double-check all of her results with a second, carefully calibrated set of equipment. But then there were scientists who didn't like her. Marie was shocked when Antoine-Herman de Barrande informed her that Radium is not an element, and that no element had yet been discovered. Many other physicists and chemists showed similar skepticism, suggesting that she was getting nonsense results and imagining that she had detected a new element. "Their work and theories are not true," Marie stated. "Radium is a natural element. Because they want to protect their own._

I was particularly impressed with the kindness and humility Euler showed toward me, especially given my… questionable contributions to humanity. He encouraged me to dream big and think of problems big and small, no matter how small. No matter what my engineering qualifications, I would be able to contribute to science, even if it was just by following the laws of nature.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Ladies and Gentlemen, welcome to our special dinner party tonight! We are honored to share a meal with such notable guests as Cleopatra, Dicken's Charles Dickens, Bob Pettit and President John F. Kennedy. So, let's get started!

Charles Dickens: (clears throat) Well, well, well, Bob Pettit. Surprised to see you at my dinner party!

Bob Pettit: Ya know, Chuck, I've got to admit you snuck me up here. No idea how I got invited to this dinner.

President John F. Kennedy: Of course you made it, Bob. You were an iconic player and an amazing person. Charles, after reading 'A Christmas Carol', no one had the right to drink so much brandy at midnight!

Bob Pettit: (chuckles) That's right, Chuck. To being a good man and never giving up!

Charles Dickens: I have to say, J.F.K., you really were one of the greatest people to ever walk this earth. Your presidency was full of so much history and change. But you know, if you had a chance to do it again, what would you change?

President John F. Kennedy: That's a great question, Chuck. (turns to Cleopatra) Cleo, I'm sure you've seen your fair share of history. President of the University of Mexico, a few lands at Texas A&M, and a tenured professor at Stanford. But if you had the chance to make changes, what would be your first move?

Cleopatra: (leans back, sipping wine) Impressive, J.F.K. I must agree with you. However long you think I ruled, it was not long enough. When I was young, I was the strongest and greatest of rulers, yet still lost my empire and my life. My power did not come from the throne, it came from me. So, if I had the chance to make one change, I would've secured my position by marrying Caesar.

Charles Dickens: (laughs) A political marriage, my dear? Well, Cleo, believe me when I say that no matter your age, there is nothing wrong with a little love.

President John F. Kennedy: (nodding) Of course there's not. Love can accomplish so many things, lovers. I, for one, believe in the power of love. (winks)

Host: Well, that's all the time we have for dinner, folks. I hope you enjoyed sharing this meal with each other as much as we enjoyed watching you. Thank you for coming tonight.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would avoid Jesus, Muhammad, and Buddha because of the sensitive nature of inviting religious figures. But there are plenty of others to choose from.

I would have the following guests at my dinner party:

1. Isaac Newton (1642-1726) - Sir Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, astronomer, and physicist. Known for his contributions to the Laws of Motion and Universal Gravitation, Newton was an influential figure in the scientific revolution. 

2. Johann Wolfgang Von Goethe (1749-1832) - Johann Wolfgang von Goethe was a German writer and statesman who also served as minister of state for the duchy of Saxe-Weimar. Widely regarded as one of the greatest poets and playwrights in all of German literature, von Goethe authored iconic works such as "Faust" and "The Sorrows of Young Werther."

3. Marie Curie (1867-1934) - Marie Curie was a French physicist and chemist who became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize in 1903 for her pioneering work on radioactivity. She went on to earn a second Nobel Prize in 1911, this time in chemistry, for her discovery of radium and polonium. 

4. Ada Lovelace (1815-1852) - Ada Lovelace was a British mathematician known for her work on Charles Babbage's early mechanical general-purpose computer, the Analytical Engine. Lovelace is widely regarded as the first computer programmer, having written the first algorithm intended to be processed by a machine.

5. Ida B. Wells (1862-1931) - Ida B. Wells was a black journalist, suffragist, and social activist in Memphis, Tennessee. Born a slave, Wells went on to become a leading figure in the anti-lynching movement, using her platform as a newspaper editor to expose the brutal treatment of African Americans at the hands of racist mobs and the justice system.

Olivia Newton John, in your opinion, what's the importance of music in a person's life?

I love Sara Bareilles' voice. I mean, it's magical. There are so many ways that your voice can change and grow and be used to help people. What inspired you to start singing?

The toaster was on sale at Target for 50 dollars, and I thought, "Wow, I could get a toaster now!" Cleansing Power of Earth was a woman who spent years in Tibet meditating to improve the minds of her people. She was a disciple of Milarepa and was regarded as one of his most potent daughters, capable of curing disease and taming wild animals through the power of prayer. She died in 1960.

Alexander Friedmann was a Russian physicist and cosmologist who made fundamental contributions to the theory of the expanding universe. He died in 1925.

Mary Jackson was an American aerospace engineer and the first Black female engineer at NASA. She died in 2005.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
As a goddess, I am able to host an elaborate dinner for myself with the figures of Cleopatra VII, Beyoncé, and Marilyn Monroe.
While dining, I chat with the three women about my life. 

Cleopatra seems especially delighted to chat with me, as she is honored and flattered by the idea of getting to dine with a goddess. She tells me all about the lessons she learned from her upbringing as a member of the dynasty that ruled Egypt more than 2000 years ago.

Beyoncé immediately pops a bottle of expensive champagne for me. She's so excited to be at my dinner party and cannot wait to gush about her accomplishments in music, dance, and film. She's got a lot of business to tend to, though, so she tells me that she's got to jet out after dinner to go to a concert rehearsal.

Marilyn is thrilled to be at my dinner party too. She giggles merrily and shares a story about appearing in a film classic with my half-brother Dionysus. She believes that she will be at my dinner party for the rest of eternity, but I show her a calendar and say "The evening is only scheduled until 10PM." She pouts, but has to reluctantly agree that she needs to get ready for bed.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The table was set for dinner in a large dining room, elegantly decorated with draperies and candles. The room was warm, and the scents of herbs and spices filled the air.

I walked through the room to greet my guests, making conversation with each one. There were so many people I enjoyed talking to. Wearing a gown of blue and gold, Mary, Queen of Scots sat at one end of the table. At the other end, Leonardo da Vinci sat sketching. To my left, Florence Nightingale spoke of her work, and to my right, Leonardo di Caprio regaled us with stories from his recent film, "Dunkirk."

"I am honored to have you all here," I said, raising my glass. We all clinked our glasses in celebration.

"I can't wait to hear Leonardo tell stories of his time as an actor," said Mary, Queen of Scots.

"Is it true you painted with your left hand?" Leonardo da Vinci asked Leonardo di Caprio with a smile.

Leonardo di Caprio blushed. "Uh, I don't... I could try." He picked up a paintbrush and began to sketch.

"You see, I like to paint with my left hand," Leonardo da Vinci said, looking at Leonardo di Caprio's sketch. "My brain is structured in a different way than most people's. The left side is where my creativity comes from."

"Leonardo was a gifted painter," Florence Nightingale said, lighting a candle. "He was a true artist."

Mary, Queen of Scots nodded in agreement. "And he was a true Renaissance man. He was a sculptor, an inventor, an anatomist, a musician. And he didn't shy away from new ideas."

Leonardo da Vinci smiled, pleased to hear such praise for his work.

Throughout the night, we laughed and talked, exchanging stories and ideas. It was a night I would never forget.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For my special dinner party, I would choose Napoleone di Buonaparte, Aristotle, Marie Curie, William Shakespeare and Rumi.

Napoleon was one of the greatest military leaders in history, and I would love to hear his strategies and theories of war. Aristotle was a towering genius and a philosopher who influenced the intellectual development of Europe – I would be very interested in learning his thoughts.

Marie Curie was a Polish physicist, chemist and radioactivity researcher – she was a pioneer in her field, and it would be interesting to hear how she persevered and she overcame the obstacles. William Shakespeare was, and still is, one of the greatest writers in English language – I would love to learn how he came up with his unique writing style and characters.

Lastly, I would choose Rumi, the Persian poet, Sufi mystic and Sufi teacher, who is internationally recognized and regarded as one of the greatest poets of all time. I would be keen to hear his beliefs and ideas on love and spirituality. It would be an incredible experience to meet these boundary-breaking individuals and hear their stories.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Welcome everyone to our dinner party! Our guests tonight include Marie Curie, Albert Einstein, and Isaac Newton.
Marie Curie: Thank you for having me. I'm excited to meet some of the greatest minds of our time.
Albert Einstein: Yes, it's quite an honor. It's been a while since I've spoken with Dr. Curie.
Isaac Newton: Indeed. I have always been a great admirer of yours, Marie.
Marie Curie: And I, of yours, Dr. Newton. Your work on gravity has been a great influence on me.
Albert Einstein: Speaking of gravity, I wanted to talk to you, Newton, about your ideas on the topic.
Isaac Newton: Oh, certainly. I have been thinking a great deal about it myself. I think Einstein's theories are fascinating, but I am not entirely convinced by them.
Albert Einstein: Well, we can debate that at some point after dinner. I think the food looks delicious.
Marie Curie: I second that. I haven't tasted this good a meal in quite some time.
Albert Einstein: So, Marie, can you tell us a little bit about what you've been working on lately?
Marie Curie: Yes, I have been continuing my research on radioactivity. I believe there is still so much we have to learn on the topic.
Isaac Newton: It's fascinating. I have always been intrigued by the behavior of particles at the subatomic level.
Albert Einstein: Well, in that case, Newton, you should know that I have been working on a theory that could explain the behavior of those particles.
Isaac Newton: A theory? I would very much like to hear more about it.
Albert Einstein: So, to put it simply, I believe that particles can exist in multiple states at the same time, until they are observed.
Marie Curie: I have to say, that does seem quite fascinating. I have always believed that there is so much that we do not yet understand about the world.
Isaac Newton: Indeed. I think that is what makes science such an exciting field of study.
Host: So, what do you think? Would you like to continue the discussion over dinner?
Marie Curie: Of course. I think this will be a wonderful dinner party.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Host: Tonight I'm honored to have with us Jean d'Alembert, Voltaire, and Montesquieu. 

Jean d'Alembert (JD): Thank you. This is wonderful. I'm so happy to reunite with you, Voltaire. 

Voltaire (V): My dear Alembert, I couldn't agree more. JD, you have done incredible work in mathematics and physics, and I can't wait to hear more about your ideas on science and rationalism.

Host: There you are, Montesquieu! Welcome to the party. JD, V, I'd like you to meet Montesquieu.

Jean d'Alembert: Oh Montesquieu, I've only heard amazing things about your work on government.

Montesquieu (M): Thank you, I'm honored. Voltaire, you have received some criticism lately for your attacks on the church. How do you deal with that?

V: Well, I don't think it's right for the church to hold so much political power, that's all. It's dangerous. Fortunately, the king of Prussia, Fredrick the Great, is a fan of my ideas and my writing.

Montesquieu: Excellent choice of patron!

Jules Verne (JV): I agree wholeheartedly with each and all of you. 

Host: JV! You snuck in here while we weren't looking! Where have you been all day?

JV: Oh, hello everyone. I was just exploring the rooms. 

V: The new Duino Elegies. Very interesting. I plan to translate them myself and publish them in my newspaper.

JV: I'm writing a whole series about foreign countries! It's called Around the World in Eighty Days, and it's based on my belief that the world is becoming smaller...

M: Yes, the effects of globalism are undeniable. But one can't deny that Napoleon was a great military leader.

Host: That's an interesting point, M. The breadth of your influence on developing the legal precedents we still have today, your contribution from behind the scenes at court, and your philosophies on the separation of powers in government I appreciate more than you can imagine.

JV: And you were right about government! 

D]|[\[ V&M: Good night!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Aristotle, Diogenes of Sinope
Guest list: Plato, Aristotle, Diogenes of Sinope

Menu:
Starter: Sardines, rabbit, oysters
Main Course: Roast goat
Dessert: Sesame cake
Drinks:lemnosa, honey wine

Plato: Socrates never spoke to you?
Aristotle: Nein. Johann Busesch was dead.
Plato: Isn't Busesch known for - 

Diogenes: What's all this? I could stand on my head and saunter through a room, and there's no way I'd be more out of place here than I am now. No goats, no nakedness, no sunbathing - even the scent of civility is making me(...gag) ill.

Plato: What are you complaining about? We have Skype.
Diogenes: Skype?! For what? So I can turned beetroot red when I find out what that Cascian blob is trying to tell me? I'd rather spend an hour arguing with Xenocrates in person.

Aristotle: Xenocrates was my good friend...
Plato: He is widely held to have been -
Diogenes: Boring. He makes you yawn, and it's not even the brain abscesses talking.

Plato: He succeeded Speusippus as the head of the Academy.
Diogenes: (rolls eyes) Please do not tell me his ascension to such a magnificent position gave him the courage to commence his pursuits as a bold poet of the deeds of Dinarchus. 

Aristotle: DINARCHUS was a predecessor... he founded my school.
Diogenes: Aristotle, I could fill these wine barrels with all your masters' names. But would I be more impressed if I were to drink all these sickeningly sweet offerings of yours instead? 

Plato: My name is Prodicus.
Diogenes: (groans in agony) Lesche? I'm being trampled in Lesche and eaten by yourGORGOYLE! 

Plato: I am Plato.
Diogenes: Pla-to-rus! I'll bite off his ear! And his nose! And then I'll dismember him and teach that insipidist to behave like a proper, manly Cynic! 
Plato: No, my name is_______

Aristotle: (lunges forward) Speak, you Cadmean pig! Speak!
Plato: I......I am Plato. Can I continue? Well, after Speusippus you became the head...

Diogenes: Oh, wonderful! With you wheeling and dealing, our beloved school will mushroom into a garden at no time! (claps hands)

Plato: Perhaps you'll be more interested in the many philosophers you've influenced...
Diogenes: Influenced?! Influencing someone makes me feelingsnaive, and your philosophic generations are all cranks!

Aristotle: Diogenes, if I were a bad teacher, I wouldn't be a teacher at all.
Diogenes: You'd be PRISONER. Look at that! You don't take that seriously, do you? *crushes napkin* 

Plato: We are fellow Aletheans here.
Diogenes: Aletheans?! I'd sooner be Slaves on a desert island! Brother, the Practice of HONESTY is now and forevermore a foreign language to me! (hyperventilates)
Aristotle: Diogenes are you breathing alright? Need some ovation?

Plato: You must rest now.
Diogenes: I won't touch this abomination with the tip of my wrist, let alone my head! (stretches)

Plato: Do you have any final words for us?
Diogenes: Comfortable position? Stand up. (adjusts chair) 

Plato: Aristotle, Diogenes enjoyed our discussion. He praises your respect for
Aristotle: And Mentor, I thank you for your...'
Aristotle: ... Me? I was there for a whole five minutes. (chuckles)
Aristotle: Plato, I may have things to thank you for as well. (leans back)
Plato: I think you've had your fun. Diogenes, we'll continue our assembly after you get some rest.
Diogenes: (sighs) Yes, Myra, yes...

Plato: Aristotle, can I have a word with you.
Aristotle: Yes, please. (whispers) He's been asleep for hours.
Plato: I appreciate the parody, but-
Aristotle: It's an art form.

Plato: *sarcastic* Ah... art. That's what it is. Capitalism of ideals. Cradle to grave Surveillance of Morality and Ideological purity. 
 
Aristotle: Whoa whoa whoa. That's a little harsh, no?
Plato: I've seen it! I've seen people being educated as slaves allying with their oppressors!
Aristotle: Idealism has consequences, yes. But it's a necessary evil.
Plato: Necessary EVIL?!

Aristotle: Diogenes asked me earlier what my school was called, and I told him. He was unhappy about that, it seems.
Plato: I understand. He's far more than an acquaintance of mine. He's a Jew. 
Aristotle: ...Jew? I've never heard of him being religious.
Plato: He is from Sinope. He is a Sinopean Citizen. (buries head in hands) 

Aristotle: (laugh) I've never known an argument to be so... artistic. So unpredictable. (waves)--OK, guys! It's time to wrap things up. Have a great day, fill out your evaluations on the way out, and Happy Hour starts in ten. And that's not a metaphor. (claps)

Plato: I won't see him anymore. 
Aristotle: Plato, I can't even remotely understand your anger. 
Plato: Diogenes isn't an angry man.
Aristotle: He was under a lot of stress, losing a legal battle and all.
Plato: The trial was incompetence on his part, that's true. 

Aristotle: But homelessness? Over-reacting is one thing, but that's becoming institutionalized!
Plato: Diogenes was unhappy with the way his followers operated.
Aristotle: Did he ever speak to you about it?
Plato: No. We argued...
Aristotle: I wish I were there to at least hear your side of it.
Plato: I don't need your sympathy, Aristotle...
Aristotle: Plato, I'm just trying to help.
Plato: We were Debating...
Aristotle: I know that. But surely someone needed to figure out what was best for Diogenes.
Plato: When Diogenes tries to fix a problem, five more manifest themselves. That's a vice that he's trying to overcome. He needs my guidance and criticism in order to grow. 
Aristotle: But I thought you were a friend. 
Plato: He has his own friends. He's not alone in this.
Aristotle: Then why are you upset?
Plato: He said we were losing touch.
Aristotle: He said it,
Aristotle: But you didn't respond.
Plato: I told you that I couldn't-
Aristotle: Because you didn't care.
Plato: Because---
Aristotle: It's over, Plato.
Plato: *indignant* What do you-
Aristotle: You need to pull yourself together. If Diogenes asked me to take your place as a mentor, I'd do it in a heartbeat.
Plato: *shocked* You have no idea what you're even saying, let alone doing.
Aristotle: I know exactly what I'm doing, and I know that if I didn’t do it, it wouldn't work.
Plato: *stern* You have no right to decide that for me.
Aristotle: *cold* You have no right to decide what's best for him.
Plato: *soft* I tried...
Aristotle: *empty* ...And it was never enough.
Plato: *melancholy* You don't understand. I love him. 
Aristotle: *pitiless* Good. You chose me. So leave me to be the one to fix it.
Plato: *silent* ...
Aristotle: *cold* Bye.
Plato: *collapsed* ... (bursts into tears) 

Diogenes: Yo! I only snore because of you!
Diogenes: (jumps out of bed) Why are you cryingyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyyy nooooooooooooooo! (hugs)

Plato: My name is Plato.
Diogenes: Name's Diogenes.
Plato: And I want to redeem myself.
Diogenes: ...And I want to eat a live chicken. 
Plato: We'll start (expiring)
Diogenes: Yeah baby! You rock{(awkwardly slides off bed)

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Seeing some of the world’s most influential figures in one place, all in conversation with one another, sounds like a sight to behold. 

At my special dinner party, I would invite Isaac Newton, Frank Lloyd Wright, Marie Curie, and Clara Barton. 

Newton and Wright in particular I’d love to hear talk about, as they were both visionaries in creating something previously unseen. I’d love to know how each tackled obstacle after obstacle, never giving up until he found a final solution. Their stories of perseverance could certainly find common ground, and I look forward to hearing what they have to say about one another as well.

On the other hand, Marie Curie and Clara Barton are the kinds of people who made a difference in other people’s lives directly. Curie discovered radiation; she was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the first person and only woman to win in two fields. Clara Barton was an advocate for the sick and wounded, especially the soldiers of the Civil War. Their contributions brought about positive change, and I would be in awe of hearing how Barton truly “never missed an opportunity to do a good thing”. 

As each of these individuals had an unseen commonality — being change makers in their own unique ways — I’m excited to find out what they’d have to say together.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The bustle of activity seemed to rattle through the walls of the banquet hall, clanging of dishes, popping of parchments, excited squeaks of laughter—simple sounds that contrasted with the formal decor. Mosaics of Roman gods and heroes flashed azure in the torchlight, and the acrid whiff of burning sulfur permeated the air.

Menas of Alexandria lifted his slender fingers to summon a goblet of wine, and wove a hand over his piercing storm-colored eyes. He silently waited till a shadow looming over the grouping of Nobles to his right was closer before taking a sip from his chalice. Polyaenus of Skepsis and Menachem ben Sarbati passed through the hall with an entourage of armed retainers: Menas had saved the lives of both of them and gifted each of them vast tracts of land to mark his generosity.

Once the guards reached the door, Polyaenus approached. "Menas," he greeted warmly, nodding to the slave trader, "I see preparation has already begun!"

"Of course," Menas replied with a cold smile, despite his gratitude. "There is much to look forward to."

Polyaenus took a seat on the bench next to the Celtiberian, hauling an armchair up onto his lap. "You can not imagine how restless I've been, Menas," Polyaenus confided, "My whole army has clamored for me ever since I heard the rumors of your incredible success."

Menas placed his chalice on the edge of the table, and scanned his joiner for his next guest. After a moment of consideration, a raven-haired woman walked through the door, adjusting the nightdress and veil she wore. She was a beautiful sight, and certainly no man in the room would dedicate a glimmer of unwanted attention to Polyaenus. Menas almost groaned. Why hadn't she come on time as he had instructed?

Before Menas could open his mouth, Polyaenus spoke. "Is that Nusayra? She has grown into such a lovely woman!"

Menas sighed, pushing the armchair away from his partner to create room on the bench. "Polyaenus, it's late," he snapped. "We have a dinner to prepare and—"

Suddenly, Menachem ben Sarbati entered the hall. His thundering steps echoed through the speaker's podiums and carpeted dining area, and the soldiers at the door tensed, their weapons clattering together. Menas looked over his shoulder, steeling himself. Menachem had a temper—Menas knew better than to accuse him of anything.

As usual, however, the Arab conqueror was not his true self whenever he was with his confidants. Menas only needed to turn his head around. And there she was. In an azure veil and a bold red nightdress, Nusayra was a perfect counterpart to Menas. She walked windmilling through the crowd without a care, until she reached Menas and gazed into his eyes. He couldn't turn her away—not after his grand plans for her, or Polyaenus for that matter.

"And what is this commotion, Menas?" she asked, her voice sharp as ever.

Polyaenus and Menachem burst into laughter behind him.

"Menachem! Polyaenus!" Menas replied, winking at them. "This is Nusayra, my mother and general."

Nusayra's niệm?? eyebrows dipped, and she pursed her lips, but Menas opened his arms. "Nusayra. I'd like you to meet my comrade and friend, Polyaenus of Skepsis."

"Charmed," Polyaenus answered genially. "And how do ambassadors greet each other in your lands?"

"Give me your right hand," Nusayra commanded, "in meek service."

Polyaenus reached out tentatively and took the woman's hand in his, and she smiled coldly, revealing a hooked fang of a front tooth. "You have a splendid voice, Polyaenus. It would be fitting for a good stage play."

"Thank you," Polyaenus replied with a nervous smile. "Speaking of which, there is a performance set for tomorrow night. The acting is exquisite, Menas—the original heroes are hardly able to keep up!"

Menas raised an eyebrow in spite of himself. "Is that so? I'll have to get some soldiers to find out who these actors are."

Nusayra took advantage of Menas's distracted chatter. She turned to Polyaenus and held out her other hand, gesturing for him to come closer. "Join us at our table, Polyaenus," she instructed, her voice dripping with danger, "There are matters to discuss involving every general in the area."

Polyaenus paused, wanting to show loyalty to Menas, but not wanting to anger the woman he was beginning to fear. Finally he nodded cautiously, and slid onto the bench beside Menachem.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd invite Albert Einstein, Marie Curie and Michael Faraday to this dinner party.

"Good evening ladies and gentlemen, I'd like to start the evening by welcoming Albert Einstein, Marie Curie and Michael Faraday to my home. Albert, can you share some thoughts on your theories of relativity?"

Albert Einstein: (smiling) Thank you. I believe that my theories concern themselves with the relationships between space, time, matter, energy, and gravity. They suggest that the laws of physics may be different for different observers, depending on their state of motion relative to other objects.

"And Marie, what was your greatest invention in science? Faraday, how has your work influenced the modern electrical industry?"

Marie Curie: In 1903, my husband Pierre and I discovered radium and polonium and advanced the theory of radioactivity. The beneficent effects of radium and its compounds have lowered the co-efficient of human mortality in many cases, opening wide the future of electrical treatment of diseases.

Michael Faraday: I believe my work played a role in the development of the modern electrical industry. My experimental work opened the pathway to the question of the relation between matter and electricity considered as two manifestations of one and the same thing.

Einstein: You're correct, Michael. Through my theory of relativity, I showed that nothing could travel faster than the speed of light, not even electricity. This had a profound effect on the way we understand the laws of physics and the behavior of matter. And Marie, your work on radioactivity revealed the existence of atomic energy and, as you mention, opened up new possibilities in the field of medicine.

Curie: In some ways, Albert, your theory of relativity and the concept of mass-energy equivalence opened our eyes to a new way of thinking about the ultimate nature of reality, and perhaps this is the ultimate meaning of science.

Faraday: I believe we are allakin to the task of imposing corporate order on the chaos into which our gathered knowledges have brought us, I mean a scientific order, into which the empirical facts of human experience shall be harmoniously combined with ideas and conceptions which their discovery may have gradually suggested.

Einstein: That's an interesting concept, Michael. I think that science is ultimately about seeking to understand the fundamental workings of the universe. It's a bit like trying to put together a jigsaw puzzle. We may never know for certain what the final picture looks like, but by continuing to gather information and test hypotheses, we can come closer to the truth. Marie, what are your thoughts on that?

Curie: Yes, that is an important idea. I believe that the ultimate goal of science is to discover the underlying principles that govern the behavior of the universe. For me, that meant studying the properties of radioactivity and discovering the elements radium and polonium. But I also came to see that scientific discovery was not just about uncovering new knowledge, but also about using that knowledge to solve practical problems and improve the human condition. It is together, with Manhattan crowd and spirit, that the modern man can become what he wishes to be.

Faraday: The greatest of all physical truths, the germ of the Universe lies in small things and if we may express the admiration that we entertain for them in an exaggerated form, in their smallest things, they are the greatest, because they are the germs of all the greatest ones.
